title: Christina Aguilera

infobox.value: Christina María Aguilera   (1980-12-18) December 18, 1980 (age 45)  [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), US

* [Discography](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera_discography "Christina Aguilera discography") * [songs](/wiki/List_of_songs_recorded_by_Christina_Aguilera "List of songs recorded by Christina Aguilera") * [videography](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera_videography "Christina Aguilera videography") * [concerts](/wiki/List_of_Christina_Aguilera_concerts "List of Christina Aguilera concerts") and [tours](/wiki/List_of_Christina_Aguilera_concert_tours "List of Christina Aguilera concert tours")
[Full list](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Christina_Aguilera "List of awards and nominations received by Christina Aguilera")

([/ˌæɡɪˈlɛərə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*AG-il-AIR-ə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), Spanish: [[kɾisˈtina maˈɾi.a aɣiˈleɾa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish");[[1]](#cite_note-1) born December 18, 1980) is an American singer, songwriter, actress, and television personality. Recognized as [an influential figure in music](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Christina_Aguilera "Cultural impact of Christina Aguilera") and having received [widespread public interest](/wiki/Public_image_of_Christina_Aguilera "Public image of Christina Aguilera"), she is noted for her four-[octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") [vocal range](/wiki/Vocal_range "Vocal range") extending into the [whistle register](/wiki/Whistle_register "Whistle register"), artistic reinventions, and incorporating controversial themes into her music. Referred to as the "[Voice of a Generation](/wiki/Honorific_nicknames_in_popular_music "Honorific nicknames in popular music")", she was also named a [Disney Legend](/wiki/Disney_Legend "Disney Legend"), in recognition of her contributions to [the Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American singer and songwriter (born 1980)

For other uses, see [Christina Aguilera (disambiguation)](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera_(disambiguation) "Christina Aguilera (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Christina Aguilar](/wiki/Christina_Aguilar "Christina Aguilar").

**Christina María Aguilera** ([/ˌæɡɪˈlɛərə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*AG-il-AIR-ə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), Spanish: [[kɾisˈtina maˈɾi.a aɣiˈleɾa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish");[[1]](#cite_note-1) born December 18, 1980) is an American singer, songwriter, actress, and television personality. Recognized as [an influential figure in music](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Christina_Aguilera "Cultural impact of Christina Aguilera") and having received [widespread public interest](/wiki/Public_image_of_Christina_Aguilera "Public image of Christina Aguilera"), she is noted for her four-[octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") [vocal range](/wiki/Vocal_range "Vocal range") extending into the [whistle register](/wiki/Whistle_register "Whistle register"), artistic reinventions, and incorporating controversial themes into her music. Referred to as the "[Voice of a Generation](/wiki/Honorific_nicknames_in_popular_music "Honorific nicknames in popular music")", she was also named a [Disney Legend](/wiki/Disney_Legend "Disney Legend"), in recognition of her contributions to [the Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company").

After appearing on *[The All New Mickey Mouse Club](/wiki/The_Mickey_Mouse_Club#1989–1994_revival:_The_All_New_Mickey_Mouse_Club "The Mickey Mouse Club")* (1993–1994), Aguilera recorded the theme song, "[Reflection](/wiki/Reflection_(song) "Reflection (song)")", for the [animated film](/wiki/Animated_film "Animated film") *[Mulan](/wiki/Mulan_(1998_film) "Mulan (1998 film)")* (1998) and signed a record deal with [RCA Records](/wiki/RCA_Records "RCA Records"). She rose to fame in 1999 with her [self-titled debut album](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera_(album) "Christina Aguilera (album)"), which topped the US [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") and garnered three US [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") number-one singles: "[Genie in a Bottle](/wiki/Genie_in_a_Bottle "Genie in a Bottle")", "[What a Girl Wants](/wiki/What_a_Girl_Wants_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "What a Girl Wants (Christina Aguilera song)")" and "[Come on Over Baby (All I Want Is You)](/wiki/Come_on_Over_Baby_(All_I_Want_Is_You) "Come on Over Baby (All I Want Is You)")". Seeking a departure from her [teen idol](/wiki/Teen_idol "Teen idol") image, Aguilera took on a more provocative image on *[Stripped](/wiki/Stripped_(Christina_Aguilera_album) "Stripped (Christina Aguilera album)")* (2002), one of the [best-selling albums of the 21st century](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_albums_of_the_21st_century "List of best-selling albums of the 21st century"). After another old-school-inspired musical reinvention, she released her second number-one album in the US, *[Back to Basics](/wiki/Back_to_Basics_(Christina_Aguilera_album) "Back to Basics (Christina Aguilera album)")* (2006). Throughout these periods, she amassed various worldwide hits, including "[Lady Marmalade](/wiki/Lady_Marmalade#Moulin_Rouge!_version "Lady Marmalade")", "[Dirrty](/wiki/Dirrty "Dirrty")", "[Beautiful](/wiki/Beautiful_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Beautiful (Christina Aguilera song)")" and "[Ain't No Other Man](/wiki/Ain%27t_No_Other_Man "Ain't No Other Man")".

During the early 2010s, Aguilera featured on the worldwide top ten singles "[Moves like Jagger](/wiki/Moves_like_Jagger "Moves like Jagger")", "[Feel This Moment](/wiki/Feel_This_Moment "Feel This Moment")" and "[Say Something](/wiki/Say_Something_(A_Great_Big_World_song) "Say Something (A Great Big World song)")"; the first of these topped the Hot 100, making her the fourth woman in history to top the chart over three consecutive decades. She also starred in the film *[Burlesque](/wiki/Burlesque_(2010_American_film) "Burlesque (2010 American film)")* (2010) and contributed to [its soundtrack](/wiki/Burlesque_(soundtrack) "Burlesque (soundtrack)"), earning a [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award "Golden Globe Award") [nomination](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Original_Song "Golden Globe Award for Best Original Song"). Aguilera remained musically active, having released a total of [nine studio albums](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera_discography "Christina Aguilera discography"). Her other ventures included a role in the television series *[Nashville](/wiki/Nashville_(2012_TV_series) "Nashville (2012 TV series)")* (2015); roles in the films *[The Emoji Movie](/wiki/The_Emoji_Movie "The Emoji Movie")* (2017) and *[Zoe](/wiki/Zoe_(2018_film) "Zoe (2018 film)")* (2018); becoming an ambassador for the [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme") (WFP); co-founding the company [Playground](/wiki/Playground_(company) "Playground (company)"); and serving as a coach on the [reality competition](/wiki/Reality_competition "Reality competition") show *[The Voice](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_TV_series) "The Voice (American TV series)")* (2011–2016).

Aguilera is one of the [best-selling music artists of all time](/wiki/Best-selling_music_artists_of_all_time "Best-selling music artists of all time"), with over 100 million records sold worldwide. She has been named one of the most successful artists of the 2000s by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* and was listed among the [greatest singers of all time](/wiki/Rolling_Stone%27s_200_Greatest_Singers_of_All_Time "Rolling Stone's 200 Greatest Singers of All Time") by *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*. Aguilera has been regarded as one of the most influential [Latin American](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") artists in the [entertainment industry](/wiki/Show_business "Show business"), having helped shape the "[Latin explosion](/wiki/Latin_American_music_in_the_United_States#2000s_Latin_pop_boom "Latin American music in the United States")" in the early 2000s with *[Mi Reflejo](/wiki/Mi_Reflejo "Mi Reflejo")* (2000), the best-selling Latin pop album of that year. *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* named her one of the [100 most influential people in the world](/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100") in 2013. [Her accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Christina_Aguilera "List of awards and nominations received by Christina Aguilera") include five [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards"), two [Latin Grammy Awards](/wiki/Latin_Grammy_Awards "Latin Grammy Awards"), six [ALMA Awards](/wiki/ALMA_Award "ALMA Award"), two [MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Awards "MTV Video Music Awards"), a [*Billboard* Music Award](/wiki/Billboard_Music_Awards "Billboard Music Awards"), a [*Guinness World Record*](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records"), and a star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame").

## Early life

Christina María Aguilera was born on December 18, 1980, in [Ocean Breeze, New York](/wiki/Ocean_Breeze,_New_York "Ocean Breeze, New York"), at [Staten Island University Hospital](/wiki/Staten_Island_University_Hospital "Staten Island University Hospital"),[[2]](#cite_note-auto-2) to Shelly Loraine ([née](/wiki/N%C3%A9e "Née") Fidler) and Fausto Wagner Xavier Aguilera.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Her father is an [Ecuadorian](/wiki/Ecuadorian "Ecuadorian") emigrant from [Guayaquil](/wiki/Guayaquil "Guayaquil") while her mother has German, Irish, Welsh, and Dutch ancestry.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez20032–3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Fausto Aguilera was a [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") sergeant, and Shelly Loraine was a violinist in the [American Youth Symphony](/wiki/American_Youth_Symphony "American Youth Symphony") before becoming a Spanish translator.[[6]](#cite_note-Remade-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez20032-7)

Due to Fausto's military service, Aguilera's family moved frequently. She lived in [Grasmere, New York](/wiki/Grasmere,_New_York "Grasmere, New York") before moving to[[2]](#cite_note-auto-2) [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") and [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas").[[8]](#cite_note-FastTrack-8) In 1983, they moved to Japan and lived in [Sagamihara](/wiki/Sagamihara "Sagamihara") for at least two years.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez20034-9) During her youth in the [Pittsburgh metropolitan area](/wiki/Pittsburgh_metropolitan_area "Pittsburgh metropolitan area"), Aguilera attended [North Allegheny Intermediate High School](/wiki/North_Allegheny_Intermediate_High_School "North Allegheny Intermediate High School") before leaving there to be homeschooled to avoid bullying she experienced at school.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonovan20108-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1986, the family returned to the United States, and settled in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), where they welcomed her younger sister, Rachel, in 1986.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez20038-12) Aguilera has spoken out about her father's physically and emotionally abusive behavior.[[13]](#cite_note-OldSoul-13) She noted that this is what made her turn to music, noting that, "growing up in an unstable environment and whatnot, music was my only real escape".[[14]](#cite_note-ChildStar-14) In 1987, Shelly filed to divorce Fausto and moved with Aguilera and Rachel to her mother's home in [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_Pennsylvania "Rochester, Pennsylvania"), a suburb of [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh").[[15]](#cite_note-15) She later married James Kearns and had a son with him named Michael.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonovan20109-16) In 2012, following decades of estrangement, Aguilera expressed interest in reconciling with her biological father.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Aguilera moving to her grandmother's home allowed her to explore her grandmother's records, which featured mostly soul and blues singers and increased her interest in music.[[18]](#cite_note-18) She also began to practice singing in public and competing in talent contests.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez200314–16-19) Following numerous contests, she earned a reputation in her neighborhood as the "little girl with a big voice" and received widespread attention from local television and radio programs.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonovan201013-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In 1990, she performed the popular song "[A Sunday Kind of Love](/wiki/A_Sunday_Kind_of_Love "A Sunday Kind of Love")" on the [reality competition](/wiki/Reality_competition "Reality competition") show *[Star Search](/wiki/Star_Search "Star Search")*, but was eliminated during the semi-final round.[[22]](#cite_note-Vault-22) Aguilera was eventually invited to sing "[The Star-Spangled Banner](/wiki/The_Star-Spangled_Banner "The Star-Spangled Banner")" before [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins") hockey, [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") football, and [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates") baseball games, and at the [1992 Stanley Cup Finals](/wiki/1992_Stanley_Cup_Finals "1992 Stanley Cup Finals").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonovan20107-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Career

### 1993–1998: Career beginnings

In 1991, Aguilera auditioned for the television series *[The All New Mickey Mouse Club](/wiki/The_All_New_Mickey_Mouse_Club "The All New Mickey Mouse Club")* (MMC), which aired on the [Disney Channel](/wiki/Disney_Channel "Disney Channel"). She ran against 400 candidates, and while she made the shortlist, she was ultimately rejected for not meeting the minimum age requirement.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez200317-25) One year later, in 1992, Aguilera received a call from one of the show's producers asking if she was still interested in becoming a "Mouseketeer". She once again competed for a spot (this time, against 15,000 candidates) and was selected to join the variety program the following year.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez200321-26) Her fellow cast members included [Ryan Gosling](/wiki/Ryan_Gosling "Ryan Gosling"), [Keri Russell](/wiki/Keri_Russell "Keri Russell"), [Britney Spears](/wiki/Britney_Spears "Britney Spears"), [Justin Timberlake](/wiki/Justin_Timberlake "Justin Timberlake"), and [JC Chasez](/wiki/JC_Chasez "JC Chasez").[[27]](#cite_note-27) During the show recordings—which included Aguilera performing [musical numbers](/wiki/Musical_number "Musical number") and [comedy sketches](/wiki/Comedy_sketch "Comedy sketch")—she moved with her family to [Orlando, Florida](/wiki/Orlando,_Florida "Orlando, Florida").[[28]](#cite_note-ProudLatina-28) In 1995, it was reported the series would not return for a new season.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez200331–32-29)

Aiming to begin a music career, Aguilera moved to Japan in 1997. She was selected to record a duet with Japanese singer [Keizo Nakanishi](/wiki/Keizo_Nakanishi "Keizo Nakanishi"), with whom she performed in concert shows around the country.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGovan201324-30) Their song, "All I Wanna Do", was released as a single but failed to reach commercial success.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonovan201013-20)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) In June 1997, Aguilera went on to Romania to represent the United States in a singers contest during the [Golden Stag Festival](/wiki/Golden_Stag_Festival "Golden Stag Festival"), failing to win over the audience.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGovan201324–25-33)

Seeking a recording contract, Aguilera recorded numerous [demo tapes](/wiki/Demo_tapes "Demo tapes") directed to record labels, including [Walt Disney Records](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Records "Walt Disney Records"), for which she sent a cover of "[Run to You](/wiki/Run_to_You_(Whitney_Houston_song) "Run to You (Whitney Houston song)")" by [Whitney Houston](/wiki/Whitney_Houston "Whitney Houston").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez200343–44-34) She eventually was chosen to record "[Reflection](/wiki/Reflection_(song) "Reflection (song)")", the theme song from the animated film *[Mulan](/wiki/Mulan_(1998_film) "Mulan (1998 film)")* (1998), which reached number 15 on the [*Billboard* Adult Contemporary](/wiki/Hot_Adult_Contemporary_Tracks "Hot Adult Contemporary Tracks") chart.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez200345-35) Following the attention she received with "Reflection", Aguilera caught the ear of [Ron Fair](/wiki/Ron_Fair "Ron Fair"), the [A&R](/wiki/A%26R "A&R") executive from [RCA Records](/wiki/RCA_Records "RCA Records"), who consequently signed Aguilera to the label.[[8]](#cite_note-FastTrack-8) In late 1998, she began to record her debut studio album, for which producers reportedly invested over $1 million worth of writers, producers and vocal lessons.[[8]](#cite_note-FastTrack-8)

### 1999–2001: *Christina Aguilera*, *Mi Reflejo*, and *My Kind of Christmas*

In May 1999, Aguilera released "[Genie in a Bottle](/wiki/Genie_in_a_Bottle "Genie in a Bottle")", the lead single off her long-awaited debut album, which topped the US [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") for five consecutive weeks and became the second best-selling single of 1999.[[36]](#cite_note-bb2000-36) The song became an international success, increasing Aguilera's popularity worldwide, topping the charts in over 20 countries.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The single also attracted the attention of conservatives including celebrities such as [Debbie Gibson](/wiki/Debbie_Gibson "Debbie Gibson") that spoke out against its lyrical content, and was eventually considered "too provocative" to be sung by a [teen idol](/wiki/Teen_idol "Teen idol").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez200370–72-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) Due to the criticism, [Radio Disney](/wiki/Radio_Disney "Radio Disney") replaced the song with a censored version.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The song was nominated for a [Grammy Award for Best Female Pop Vocal Performance](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Female_Pop_Vocal_Performance "Grammy Award for Best Female Pop Vocal Performance").[[41]](#cite_note-grammycnn-41)

Aguilera's self-titled debut album, *[Christina Aguilera](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera_(album) "Christina Aguilera (album)")*, was released on August 24, 1999, to critical praise, debuting at number one on the US [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200").[[42]](#cite_note-15Y-42) It catapulted Aguilera into fame globally and sold over ten million copies in its first year.[[43]](#cite_note-43) It was later certified eight times platinum by the [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") (RIAA),[[44]](#cite_note-RIAA-44) and it has sold over 17 million copies worldwide.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) Originally, Aguilera's desire was to create material inspired by [R&B](/wiki/R%26B "R&B") and [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), but the label opted for a more [teen pop](/wiki/Teen_pop "Teen pop") production due the genre's high financial return in the late 1990s.[[47]](#cite_note-Stories-47) At the [42nd Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/42nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "42nd Annual Grammy Awards"), Aguilera won the [Best New Artist](/wiki/Best_New_Artist "Best New Artist") category for which *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* credited the award for "[helping] certify her credentials as a real singer".[[48]](#cite_note-Time2000-48)

> I was completely blown away, shocked, overwhelmed and thrilled. I didn't expect it. I've dreamed of that since I was eight years old. I was rambling off the top of my head, my knees were shaking and I'm still floating on air because of it!

—Aguilera on winning [Best New Artist](/wiki/Best_New_Artist "Best New Artist") at the [42nd Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/42nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "42nd Annual Grammy Awards").[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGovan201343-49)

After the album's release, "[What a Girl Wants](/wiki/What_a_Girl_Wants_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "What a Girl Wants (Christina Aguilera song)")", topped the Hot 100 and is recognized as the first new number one entry on the chart for the 2000s decade.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez200388-50) The song was also nominated for [Best Female Pop Vocal Performance](/wiki/Best_Female_Pop_Vocal_Performance "Best Female Pop Vocal Performance") at the [43rd Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/43rd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "43rd Annual Grammy Awards").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez200388-50) This was followed with "[I Turn to You](/wiki/I_Turn_to_You_(All-4-One_song)#Christina_Aguilera_version "I Turn to You (All-4-One song)")" which reached number 3,[[42]](#cite_note-15Y-42) and "[Come on Over Baby (All I Want Is You)](/wiki/Come_on_Over_Baby_(All_I_Want_Is_You) "Come on Over Baby (All I Want Is You)")" which became Aguilera's third number one song and achieved worldwide success.[[51]](#cite_note-Ranked-51) She also released a cover of "[The Christmas Song](/wiki/The_Christmas_Song#Other_versions "The Christmas Song")" in November 1999, which peaked at number 18 and became the second-highest charting position of the song after the original in 1944.[[52]](#cite_note-allmusic-52)

In January 2000, Aguilera performed at the [Super Bowl XXXIV halftime show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXIV_halftime_show "Super Bowl XXXIV halftime show") alongside [Enrique Iglesias](/wiki/Enrique_Iglesias "Enrique Iglesias"), [Phil Collins](/wiki/Phil_Collins "Phil Collins") and [Toni Braxton](/wiki/Toni_Braxton "Toni Braxton").[[53]](#cite_note-53) In May, she embarked on her debut concert tour, [Christina Aguilera in Concert](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera_in_Concert "Christina Aguilera in Concert"), which toured North America, Latin America, Europe, and Japan until February 2001.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez2003169-55) Her success continued to rise with the release of her second studio album, *[Mi Reflejo](/wiki/Mi_Reflejo "Mi Reflejo")*, in September 2000 which topped both the [*Billboard* Top Latin Albums](/wiki/Billboard_Top_Latin_Albums "Billboard Top Latin Albums") and [Latin Pop Albums](/wiki/Billboard_Latin_Pop_Albums "Billboard Latin Pop Albums") for nineteen consecutive weeks.[[56]](#cite_note-yearend2000-56) The album featured Spanish-language versions of several songs from her debut album along with new songs, and had [Latin pop](/wiki/Latin_pop "Latin pop") themes.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Three singles were release for the album including the Spanish version of "Come on Over Baby (All I Want Is You)" titled "[Ven Conmigo (Solamente Tú)](/wiki/Ven_Conmigo_(Solamente_T%C3%BA) "Ven Conmigo (Solamente Tú)")", "[Pero Me Acuerdo de Ti](/wiki/Pero_Me_Acuerdo_de_Ti#Christina_Aguilera_version "Pero Me Acuerdo de Ti")" and "[Falsas Esperanzas](/wiki/Falsas_Esperanzas "Falsas Esperanzas")".[[58]](#cite_note-debut-58) The latter two were performed at the [43rd Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/43rd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "43rd Annual Grammy Awards").[[59]](#cite_note-59) The album went on to be the best-selling Latin pop album of 2000 and was later certified [six times Latin platinum](/wiki/RIAA_certification#Spanish "RIAA certification") by the RIAA.[[28]](#cite_note-ProudLatina-28)[[60]](#cite_note-60) *Mi Reflejo* was also certified Platinum in Argentina, Mexico, and Spain.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGovan201375-61) At the [2nd Annual Latin Grammy Awards](/wiki/2nd_Annual_Latin_Grammy_Awards "2nd Annual Latin Grammy Awards"), the album won [Best Female Pop Vocal Album](/wiki/Best_Female_Pop_Vocal_Album "Best Female Pop Vocal Album").[[62]](#cite_note-62)

In October 2000, Aguilera also released her third studio album, *[My Kind of Christmas](/wiki/My_Kind_of_Christmas "My Kind of Christmas")*, her first [Christmas album](/wiki/Christmas_album "Christmas album"), which reached number one on the US [Top Holiday Albums](/wiki/Top_Holiday_Albums "Top Holiday Albums") chart.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) The album received generally polarized reviews at the time but has since gone on to retrospectively receive praise.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Aguilera starred in a holiday special, *[My Reflection](/wiki/My_Reflection "My Reflection")*, which aired on December 3, 2000, on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company").[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez2003181-66) Aguilera's commercial success saw her being named the 2000 Top Female Pop Act by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")*.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The same year, she also filed a [fiduciary duty](/wiki/Fiduciary_duty "Fiduciary duty") against manager Steve Kurtz for "improper, undue, and inappropriate influence over her professional activities". She eventually hired [Irving Azoff](/wiki/Irving_Azoff "Irving Azoff") to manage her career, aiming for control of her career and image.[[68]](#cite_note-kurtz1-68)

On January 16, 2001, Aguilera featured on [Ricky Martin](/wiki/Ricky_Martin "Ricky Martin")'s "[Nobody Wants to Be Lonely](/wiki/Nobody_Wants_to_Be_Lonely "Nobody Wants to Be Lonely")", which topped charts internationally and peaked at number 13 on the Hot 100, becoming her fifth top-20 hit in the US.[[69]](#cite_note-Hot100-69) She was also listed as one of the most successful artists on the *Billboard* 200, Hot 100, and [Mainstream Top 40](/wiki/Mainstream_Top_40 "Mainstream Top 40") charts—for which the latter she was ranked among the greatest of all time.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-history1-71) The song was ranked at number 65 on [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1")'s "100 Greatest Love Songs",[[72]](#cite_note-72) and was nominated for [Best Pop Collaboration with Vocals](/wiki/Best_Pop_Collaboration_with_Vocals "Best Pop Collaboration with Vocals") at the [44th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/44th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "44th Annual Grammy Awards").[[73]](#cite_note-73) In April of that year, Aguilera featured alongside [Lil' Kim](/wiki/Lil%27_Kim "Lil' Kim"), [Mýa](/wiki/M%C3%BDa "Mýa"), and [Pink](/wiki/Pink_(singer) "Pink (singer)") on "[Lady Marmalade](/wiki/Lady_Marmalade#Moulin_Rouge!_version "Lady Marmalade")" from the [soundtrack](/wiki/Moulin_Rouge!_Music_from_Baz_Luhrmann%27s_Film "Moulin Rouge! Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film") for the film, *[Moulin Rouge!](/wiki/Moulin_Rouge! "Moulin Rouge!")* (2001).[[74]](#cite_note-74) The song received positive reviews and topped the Hot 100 for five consecutive weeks, becoming Aguilera's fourth number one.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76) The song also won the Grammy Award for Best Pop Collaboration with Vocals and the [MTV Video Music Award for Video of the Year](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Award_for_Video_of_the_Year "MTV Video Music Award for Video of the Year").[[77]](#cite_note-77)

In August 2001, [Warlock Records](/wiki/Warlock_Records "Warlock Records") released *[Just Be Free](/wiki/Just_Be_Free "Just Be Free")*, a [demo album](/wiki/Demo_album "Demo album") recorded by Aguilera between 1994 and 1995 while she was looking for a recording deal following the end of *[The All New Mickey Mouse Club](/wiki/The_All_New_Mickey_Mouse_Club "The All New Mickey Mouse Club")* (MMC).[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez2003203–206-78) She filed a suit against the label and the album's producers, aiming to stop the release of the album; however, both parties came to a confidential settlement to release the album, in which Aguilera lent out her name, likeness, and image for an unspecified amount of damages.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

### 2002–2004: New image with *Stripped*

With a new management, Aguilera started moving away from her [teen pop](/wiki/Teen_pop "Teen pop") niche and began working on a new project.[[80]](#cite_note-Puppet-80) She cultivated a new image by adopting the [alter ego](/wiki/Alter_ego "Alter ego") Xtina, dyeing her hair black, and sporting several tattoos and body piercings.[[81]](#cite_note-GotDirty-81) Aguilera's new persona was widely criticized by media outlets.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84) In September 2002, she released the controversial song, "[Dirrty](/wiki/Dirrty "Dirrty")", which garnered mixed reviews and peaked as number 48 on [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100").[[47]](#cite_note-Stories-47) The song's accompanying music video generated controversy for depicting overtly [sexual fetishes](/wiki/Sexual_fetishes "Sexual fetishes"),[[85]](#cite_note-85) and attracted the attention of conservative organizations and moralists who sought to have the video banned on [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV").[[86]](#cite_note-OnTrend-86) The video also sparked protests in Thailand and was eventually banned on the country's local television.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-Controversial-88) "Dirrty" topped the charts in the UK and Ireland,[[89]](#cite_note-89) and has gone on to become a cult classic.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Stripped_Tour_CH_-_cropped.jpg/250px-Stripped_Tour_CH_-_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stripped_Tour_CH_-_cropped.jpg)

Aguilera performing on [The Stripped Tour](/wiki/The_Stripped_Tour "The Stripped Tour") in 2003

Aguilera's fourth studio album, *[Stripped](/wiki/Stripped_(Christina_Aguilera_album) "Stripped (Christina Aguilera album)")*, followed with a release on October 22, 2002.[[91]](#cite_note-Legacy-91) She also [executive produced](/wiki/Executive_produced "Executive produced") and co-wrote the majority of the songs.[[91]](#cite_note-Legacy-91)[[92]](#cite_note-StandsUp-92) Upon release, the album received generally mixed reviews but found commercial success and debuted at number two on the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), and eventually was [certified five times platinum](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") by the RIAA.[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[44]](#cite_note-RIAA-44) In the UK, *Stripped* sold over two million copies and became the second highest-selling album by an American female artists in the 2000s.[[94]](#cite_note-2M-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95) Since then, it was reported the album sold 12 million copies globally.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

The album's second single, "[Beautiful](/wiki/Beautiful_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Beautiful (Christina Aguilera song)")", was released to widespread acclaim for its empowering lyrics about embracing inner beauty, also becoming a [LGBT anthem](/wiki/LGBT_anthem "LGBT anthem").[[97]](#cite_note-97) The song reached number two on the Hot 100 and topped the chart in various international countries, including the UK, Canada, Ireland and New Zealand.[[69]](#cite_note-Hot100-69) It also reached number one on the US [Adult Contemporary](/wiki/Adult_Contemporary_(chart) "Adult Contemporary (chart)") chart and the US [Dance Club Songs](/wiki/Dance_Club_Songs "Dance Club Songs") chart.[[98]](#cite_note-BillboardChartHistoryII-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99) At the [46th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/46th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "46th Annual Grammy Awards"), the single won [Best Female Pop Vocal Performance](/wiki/Best_Female_Pop_Vocal_Performance "Best Female Pop Vocal Performance").[[100]](#cite_note-100) This was followed with the singles — "[Fighter](/wiki/Fighter_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Fighter (Christina Aguilera song)")", "[Can't Hold Us Down](/wiki/Can%27t_Hold_Us_Down "Can't Hold Us Down")" and "[The Voice Within](/wiki/The_Voice_Within "The Voice Within")".[[71]](#cite_note-history1-71)

In June 2003, Aguilera co-headlined [The Justified & Stripped Tour](/wiki/The_Justified_%26_Stripped_Tour "The Justified & Stripped Tour") alongside [Justin Timberlake](/wiki/Justin_Timberlake "Justin Timberlake").[[101]](#cite_note-101) The joint tour visited North America solely, attracting an audience of 546,483 and grossed over $31.8 million.[[102]](#cite_note-102) It ranked sixteenth on *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")*'s list of Top 25 Tours of 2003.[[103]](#cite_note-103) Later that year, she also embarked on [The Stripped Tour](/wiki/The_Stripped_Tour "The Stripped Tour") in Europe, Asia, and Australia.[[104]](#cite_note-LiveUK-104) Her performances at the [Wembley Arena](/wiki/Wembley_Arena "Wembley Arena") were taped, broadcast on [WB Network](/wiki/WB_Network "WB Network") and eventually sold as a video album under the name *[Stripped Live in the UK](/wiki/Stripped_Live_in_the_UK "Stripped Live in the UK")*[[104]](#cite_note-LiveUK-104) In early 2004, it was reported that she would return to North America to perform a second leg of her tour but was eventually canceled due to a vocal cord injury.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

In August 2003, at the [2003 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2003_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2003 MTV Video Music Awards"), Aguilera opened the show singing "[Like a Virgin](/wiki/Like_a_Virgin_(song) "Like a Virgin (song)")" and "[Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood_(Madonna_song) "Hollywood (Madonna song)")" alongside [Britney Spears](/wiki/Britney_Spears "Britney Spears").[[106]](#cite_note-106) Halfway through the performance, she was joined by [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna") whom they both kissed, consequently making the performance highly publicized.[[107]](#cite_note-107) Various media outlets retrospectively considered it one of the "most iconic" VMAs performances of all time.[[108]](#cite_note-108) In 2008, MTV listed the performance as the number-one opening moment in the history of MTV Video Music Awards,[[109]](#cite_note-openingvma-109) while *[Blender](/wiki/Blender_(magazine) "Blender (magazine)")* magazine cited it as one of the twenty-five sexiest music moments on television history.[[110]](#cite_note-110) In November of that year, Aguilera hosted the [2003 MTV Europe Music Awards](/wiki/2003_MTV_Europe_Music_Awards "2003 MTV Europe Music Awards").[[111]](#cite_note-111) She received widespread media attention for dressing up as a [nun](/wiki/Nun "Nun") and being undressed to reveal her underclothes while performing the [slutdrop](/wiki/Slutdrop "Slutdrop") dance style.[[112]](#cite_note-XRated-112) She was later named Top Female Pop Act of 2003 by *Billboard*.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

In August 2004, Aguilera recorded a cover of "[Car Wash](/wiki/Car_Wash_(song)#Christina_Aguilera_and_Missy_Elliott_version "Car Wash (song)")" alongside rapper [Missy Elliott](/wiki/Missy_Elliott "Missy Elliott") for the [soundtrack](/wiki/Shark_Tale#Soundtrack "Shark Tale") of the [DreamWorks Animation](/wiki/DreamWorks_Animation "DreamWorks Animation") film *[Shark Tale](/wiki/Shark_Tale "Shark Tale")*.[[114]](#cite_note-114) She also voiced a Rastafarian jellyfish in the film.[[115]](#cite_note-carwash-115) That September, Aguilera was featured on "[Tilt Ya Head Back](/wiki/Tilt_Ya_Head_Back "Tilt Ya Head Back")" with rapper [Nelly](/wiki/Nelly "Nelly").[[116]](#cite_note-Memories-116) The songs respectively reached number 63 and 58 on the *Billboard* Hot 100.[[69]](#cite_note-Hot100-69)

### 2005–2008: *Back to Basics*

In early 2005, Aguilera embraced a new image inspired by [Old Hollywood](/wiki/Old_Hollywood "Old Hollywood") figures, debuting burlesque-style curly blonde hair and makeup. Her new persona was eventually adopted to promote her subsequent music project.[[117]](#cite_note-117) In March 2006, Aguilera released a duet with [Andrea Bocelli](/wiki/Andrea_Bocelli "Andrea Bocelli"), "[Somos Novios (It's Impossible)](/wiki/Somos_Novios_(It%27s_Impossible) "Somos Novios (It's Impossible)")", and performed the song together at the [Sanremo Music Festival](/wiki/Sanremo_Music_Festival "Sanremo Music Festival").[[118]](#cite_note-118) She also featured on "[A Song for You](/wiki/A_Song_for_You "A Song for You")" by [Herbie Hancock](/wiki/Herbie_Hancock "Herbie Hancock") which received a [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") nomination for [Best Pop Collaboration with Vocals](/wiki/Best_Pop_Collaboration_with_Vocals "Best Pop Collaboration with Vocals").[[119]](#cite_note-Grammy2006-119)

In June 2006, Aguilera released "[Ain't No Other Man](/wiki/Ain%27t_No_Other_Man "Ain't No Other Man")" as her then-forthcoming fifth studio album's lead single, which received critical acclaim and was praised by music critics.[[69]](#cite_note-Hot100-69) Critics compared her vocals on the songs to older singers such as [Etta James](/wiki/Etta_James "Etta James") and [Aretha Franklin](/wiki/Aretha_Franklin "Aretha Franklin").[[120]](#cite_note-120) The song achieved commercial success, peaked at number six on *Billboard* Hot 100 and has sold 1.7 million digital copies in the US[[121]](#cite_note-Shake-121) The single eventually earned her the [Grammy Award for Best Female Pop Vocal Performance](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Female_Pop_Vocal_Performance "Grammy Award for Best Female Pop Vocal Performance").[[122]](#cite_note-122)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Christina_Aguilera_at_the_MTV_Video_Music_Awards_2006.jpg/250px-Christina_Aguilera_at_the_MTV_Video_Music_Awards_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christina_Aguilera_at_the_MTV_Video_Music_Awards_2006.jpg)

Aguilera at the [2006 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2006_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2006 MTV Video Music Awards")

Aguilera's fifth studio album, *[Back to Basics](/wiki/Back_to_Basics_(Christina_Aguilera_album) "Back to Basics (Christina Aguilera album)")*, was released as a [double album](/wiki/Double_album "Double album") in August 2006.[[123]](#cite_note-Throwback-123) The album was inspired by [1920s](/wiki/1920s_in_music "1920s in music")–[1950s](/wiki/1950s_in_music "1950s in music") music and was described as a "throwback with elements of old-school [music] combined with a modern-day twist".[[124]](#cite_note-20YoX-124) The album debuted at number one on the US *Billboard* 200, marking Aguilera's second number-one album on the chart.[[125]](#cite_note-125) It also debuted atop the charts in numerous countries, including Australia, Canada, Germany, Switzerland, and the UK.[[126]](#cite_note-126) The album received positive reviews and was nominated a [Best Pop Vocal Album](/wiki/Best_Pop_Vocal_Album "Best Pop Vocal Album") at the [49th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/49th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "49th Annual Grammy Awards").[[127]](#cite_note-Grammy2007-127)[[128]](#cite_note-Greatness-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-B2BAll-130) The album was later [certified double platinum](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") by the RIAA,[[44]](#cite_note-RIAA-44) and sold over 5 million units worldwide.[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2013496-132) "[Hurt](/wiki/Hurt_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Hurt (Christina Aguilera song)")" and "[Candyman](/wiki/Candyman_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Candyman (Christina Aguilera song)")" were released as singles following the album's release and reached the top ten on various international charts, and peaked top-thirty on Hot 100.[[133]](#cite_note-133)[[69]](#cite_note-Hot100-69)

In November 2006, Aguilera performed "[Steppin' Out with My Baby](/wiki/Steppin%27_Out_with_My_Baby "Steppin' Out with My Baby")" at [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")'s *Tony Bennett: An American Classic* special as a duet alongside [Tony Bennett](/wiki/Tony_Bennett "Tony Bennett").[[134]](#cite_note-billboardsteppin-134) The duo performed the song on *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")* and at the [59th Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/59th_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "59th Primetime Emmy Awards").[[134]](#cite_note-billboardsteppin-134) Their live performance was nominated a [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Pop_Collaboration_with_Vocals "Grammy Award for Best Pop Collaboration with Vocals") at the [50th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/50th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "50th Annual Grammy Awards").[[135]](#cite_note-Grammy2008-135) Their version was officially recorded in 2012 for Bennett's *[Viva Duets](/wiki/Viva_Duets "Viva Duets")*.[[136]](#cite_note-136) That same month, Aguilera also featured on rapper [Diddy](/wiki/Diddy "Diddy")'s song "[Tell Me](/wiki/Tell_Me_(Diddy_song) "Tell Me (Diddy song)")" which topped the [UK R&B Singles chart](/wiki/UK_R%26B_Singles_and_Albums_Charts "UK R&B Singles and Albums Charts") and peaked at number eight in the UK.[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138)

At the end of November 2006 until October 2008, Aguilera embarked on the [Back to Basics Tour](/wiki/Back_to_Basics_Tour "Back to Basics Tour") which visited North America, Europe, Asia, and Oceania.[[139]](#cite_note-139) The tour received positive reviews and was divided into acts inspired by [juke joints](/wiki/Juke_joints "Juke joints") and the [circus](/wiki/Circus "Circus").[[140]](#cite_note-140) *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* highlighted its "numerous sets and costumes changes" emphasizing Aguilera's "evolution from bubblegum starlet to dirrty vixen to her current incarnation as retro-styled soul siren".[[141]](#cite_note-141) With earnings around 48.1 million, the tour was the highest-grossing concert tour by a female in 2007.[[142]](#cite_note-142) Additionally, the performances at the [Adelaide Entertainment Centre](/wiki/Adelaide_Entertainment_Centre "Adelaide Entertainment Centre") were taped, broadcast on [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1") and sold as a video album under the title *[Back to Basics: Live and Down Under](/wiki/Back_to_Basics:_Live_and_Down_Under "Back to Basics: Live and Down Under")*.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

In February 2007, Aguilera performed "[It's a Man's Man's Man's World](/wiki/It%27s_a_Man%27s_Man%27s_Man%27s_World "It's a Man's Man's Man's World")" at the [49th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/49th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "49th Annual Grammy Awards"), which received widespread praise and has been noted as one of the best Grammy performances of all time.[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145) Her performance has been voted as the 3rd Most Memorable Grammy Performance of all time, as presented in the 2007 [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") television special *[My Night at the Grammys](/wiki/My_Night_at_the_Grammys "My Night at the Grammys")*.[[146]](#cite_note-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148)[[149]](#cite_note-149) That same month she performed at the [halftime show](/wiki/Halftime_show "Halftime show") for the [2007 NBA All-Star Game](/wiki/2007_NBA_All-Star_Game "2007 NBA All-Star Game").[[150]](#cite_note-150)

In April 2008, Aguilera appeared in [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese")'s documentary, *[Shine a Light](/wiki/Shine_a_Light_(film) "Shine a Light (film)")* (which chronicled a two-day concert by [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") at the [Beacon Theatre](/wiki/Beacon_Theatre_(New_York_City) "Beacon Theatre (New York City)")), where she performed "[Live with Me](/wiki/Live_with_Me "Live with Me")" with [Mick Jagger](/wiki/Mick_Jagger "Mick Jagger").[[151]](#cite_note-151) In September, she released "[Keeps Gettin' Better](/wiki/Keeps_Gettin%27_Better "Keeps Gettin' Better")" set to feature on her first then-upcoming [greatest hits album](/wiki/Greatest_hits_album "Greatest hits album") of the same name.[[152]](#cite_note-msn-152) The song received mixed reviews and debuted at number seven on the *Billboard* Hot 100, making it her highest debut on the chart at the time,[[153]](#cite_note-153) and has since sold 1.156 million digital copies in the US.[[121]](#cite_note-Shake-121) She performed the song live at the [2008 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2008_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2008 MTV Video Music Awards") alongside a medley.[[154]](#cite_note-154) *[Keeps Gettin' Better: A Decade of Hits](/wiki/Keeps_Gettin%27_Better:_A_Decade_of_Hits "Keeps Gettin' Better: A Decade of Hits")* was released in November 2008 to positive reviews, debuting at number nine on *Billboard* 200 and was later certified gold by the RIAA.[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[44]](#cite_note-RIAA-44) The compilation added two original songs (its title track and "Dynamite") and also featured remakes of "Genie in a Bottle" and "Beautiful".[[156]](#cite_note-Hits-156)[[157]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-157) By late 2008, Aguilera stated that she had begun working on a new album.[[158]](#cite_note-158)

### 2009–2011: *Bionic*, *Burlesque* and *The Voice*

In 2009, at the end of the 2000s, Aguilera was named the twentieth best "Artist of the Decade" by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")*, and was nominated for the [MTV Europe Music Award for Best Act Ever](/wiki/List_of_MTV_Europe_Music_Award_categories#Main_categories "List of MTV Europe Music Award categories").[[69]](#cite_note-Hot100-69)[[159]](#cite_note-159) At the end of 2009, Aguilera stated that her then-forthcoming sixth studio album originally titled *Light & Darkness* would be released in March 2010.[[160]](#cite_note-160) However, by February 2010, Aguilera stated that the album would be retitled and released in June.[[161]](#cite_note-161) In April 2010, Aguilera released the lead single, "[Not Myself Tonight](/wiki/Not_Myself_Tonight "Not Myself Tonight")" which peaked at number 23 on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100").[[162]](#cite_note-single11-162)[[69]](#cite_note-Hot100-69) She performed the song live on *[The Oprah Winfrey Show](/wiki/The_Oprah_Winfrey_Show "The Oprah Winfrey Show")*.[[163]](#cite_note-163) This was followed by the album's second single, "[Woohoo](/wiki/Woohoo_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Woohoo (Christina Aguilera song)")" featuring rapper [Nicki Minaj](/wiki/Nicki_Minaj "Nicki Minaj").[[162]](#cite_note-single11-162)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Christina_Aguilera_at_the_premiere_of_Burlesque_%282010%29.jpg/250px-Christina_Aguilera_at_the_premiere_of_Burlesque_%282010%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christina_Aguilera_at_the_premiere_of_Burlesque_(2010).jpg)

Aguilera at the premiere of *[Burlesque](/wiki/Burlesque_(2010_American_film) "Burlesque (2010 American film)")* at the [Empire, Leicester Square](/wiki/Empire,_Leicester_Square "Empire, Leicester Square") in London, 2010

Aguilera released her sixth studio album, *[Bionic](/wiki/Bionic_(Christina_Aguilera_album) "Bionic (Christina Aguilera album)")*, on June 6, 2010, which debuted at number three on the *Billboard* 200, and topped the European and the UK charts.[[164]](#cite_note-164)[[165]](#cite_note-165) The album was inspired by [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music"), and was described by her as a project "about the future" noting that it was inspired by her son who motivated her to "want to play and have fun".[[166]](#cite_note-166) The album received generally mixed reviews by critics,[[162]](#cite_note-single11-162) with [Kitty Empire](/wiki/Kitty_Empire "Kitty Empire") of *[The Observer](/wiki/The_Observer "The Observer")* calling it "very strong, but only in parts".[[167]](#cite_note-167) It was later [certified gold](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") by the [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") (RIAA), and sold approximately over one million copies globally.[[44]](#cite_note-RIAA-44)[[168]](#cite_note-168) "[You Lost Me](/wiki/You_Lost_Me "You Lost Me")" and "[I Hate Boys](/wiki/I_Hate_Boys "I Hate Boys")" were released as singles in the following month of June.[[162]](#cite_note-single11-162) A tour was planned for the album, but was postponed and later canceled due to prior commitments.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

Aguilera landed her first starring role as a waitress turned [burlesque](/wiki/American_burlesque "American burlesque") performer in the [Steven Antin](/wiki/Steven_Antin "Steven Antin") film *[Burlesque](/wiki/Burlesque_(2010_American_film) "Burlesque (2010 American film)")*, released in theaters in November 2010.[[170]](#cite_note-170)[[171]](#cite_note-171) She received her star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") the same week in which the film premiered.[[172]](#cite_note-172) While the film received generally mixed reviews from critics, Aguilera's portrayal of the main character garnered positive reviews,[[173]](#cite_note-CareerBiggest-173) and the film grossed US$90 million in the box office.[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175)[[176]](#cite_note-176)[[177]](#cite_note-177)[[178]](#cite_note-178) The film also received a nomination for [Best Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy](/wiki/Best_Motion_Picture_%E2%80%93_Musical_or_Comedy "Best Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy") at the [68th Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/68th_Golden_Globe_Awards "68th Golden Globe Awards").[[179]](#cite_note-179)

In addition to starring in the film, Aguilera recorded eight tracks for the film's [accompanying soundtrack](/wiki/Burlesque:_Original_Motion_Picture_Soundtrack "Burlesque: Original Motion Picture Soundtrack"), while [Cher](/wiki/Cher "Cher") performed the remaining two.[[180]](#cite_note-180) The soundtrack reached number 18 on the *Billboard* 200 and was certified gold by the RIAA.[[44]](#cite_note-RIAA-44)[[69]](#cite_note-Hot100-69) Her song "Bound to You" received nominations for the [Golden Globe Award for Best Original Song](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Original_Song "Golden Globe Award for Best Original Song") at the [68th Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/68th_Golden_Globe_Awards "68th Golden Globe Awards") while the album itself was nominated for the [Best Compilation Soundtrack for Visual Media](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Compilation_Soundtrack_for_Visual_Media "Grammy Award for Best Compilation Soundtrack for Visual Media") at the [54th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/54th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "54th Annual Grammy Awards").[[181]](#cite_note-181) Aguilera performed "Express" on the finale of the [seventh series](/wiki/The_X_Factor_(UK_series_7) "The X Factor (UK series 7)") of *[The X Factor UK](/wiki/The_X_Factor_UK "The X Factor UK")* which received criticism for its raunchy content.[[182]](#cite_note-182)

In April 2011, Aguilera became a coach on [reality competition](/wiki/Reality_competition "Reality competition") show *[The Voice](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_TV_series) "The Voice (American TV series)")*, which aired on [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC").[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184) Aguilera later returned to its [second](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_season_2) "The Voice (American season 2)"), [third](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_season_3) "The Voice (American season 3)"), [fifth](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_season_5) "The Voice (American season 5)"), [eighth](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_season_8) "The Voice (American season 8)"), and [tenth season](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_season_10) "The Voice (American season 10)").[[185]](#cite_note-Jean-Philippe-185) For the show's [first season](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_season_1) "The Voice (American season 1)"), Aguilera's duet of her song "[Beautiful](/wiki/Beautiful_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Beautiful (Christina Aguilera song)")" with her finalist, [Beverly McClellan](/wiki/Beverly_McClellan "Beverly McClellan"), debuted at number 74 on the US [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") with first-week sales of 42,000 downloads on [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes_Store "ITunes Store").[[186]](#cite_note-186)[[187]](#cite_note-187) A year later, for the [second season](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_season_2) "The Voice (American season 2)"), Aguilera's duet of "[The Prayer](/wiki/The_Prayer_(Celine_Dion_and_Andrea_Bocelli_song) "The Prayer (Celine Dion and Andrea Bocelli song)")" with her contestant, [Chris Mann](/wiki/Chris_Mann_(singer) "Chris Mann (singer)"), also reached 85 on the chart.[[188]](#cite_note-188)

In 2011, she featured on the song "[Moves like Jagger](/wiki/Moves_like_Jagger "Moves like Jagger")" by [Maroon 5](/wiki/Maroon_5 "Maroon 5").[[189]](#cite_note-NPR12MLJ1-189) They first performed the song during their time on *The Voice* and also performed it at the [2011](/wiki/American_Music_Awards_of_2011 "American Music Awards of 2011") [American Music Awards](/wiki/American_Music_Awards "American Music Awards").[[190]](#cite_note-190)[[191]](#cite_note-mtv-191) The song received positive reviews and topped the Hot 100 for four non-consecutive weeks, making Aguilera the fourth female artist to top the chart over three decades (1990s, 2000s, and 2010s).[[192]](#cite_note-threedecadesource-192)[[193]](#cite_note-193) The song was also nominated for [Best Pop Duo/Group Performance](/wiki/Best_Pop_Duo/Group_Performance "Best Pop Duo/Group Performance") at the [54th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/54th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "54th Annual Grammy Awards").[[194]](#cite_note-Grammy2012-194) In July 2021, the song was [certified diamond](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") by the [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") (RIAA).[[195]](#cite_note-195)[[44]](#cite_note-RIAA-44)[[196]](#cite_note-196)

### 2012–2015: *Lotus* and other projects

During the [third season](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_season_3) "The Voice (American season 3)") of *The Voice* in September 2012, Aguilera debuted "[Your Body](/wiki/Your_Body_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Your Body (Christina Aguilera song)")" as the lead single from her then-upcoming seventh studio album.[[197]](#cite_note-197) The song received critical acclaim and reached number one on the US [Dance Club Song](/wiki/Dance_Club_Songs "Dance Club Songs") chart and 34 on the *Billboard* Hot 100.[[198]](#cite_note-198) The album, titled *[Lotus](/wiki/Lotus_(Christina_Aguilera_album) "Lotus (Christina Aguilera album)")*, followed in November 2012, in which Aguilera described the record as a "rebirth" of herself after the personal struggles she overcame.[[173]](#cite_note-CareerBiggest-173) Reviewers found the album generic and conventional, as opposed to Aguilera's previous experimental ventures.[[199]](#cite_note-199)[[200]](#cite_note-200) *Lotus* peaked at number 7 on the *Billboard* 200 and has sold 303,000 copies in the US as of 2019.[[201]](#cite_note-AlbumSales-201) "[Just a Fool](/wiki/Just_a_Fool "Just a Fool")" with [Blake Shelton](/wiki/Blake_Shelton "Blake Shelton") was released as the album's second single and received positive reviews.[[202]](#cite_note-202) The two performed the song live on *The Voice* and on *[The Ellen DeGeneres Show](/wiki/The_Ellen_DeGeneres_Show "The Ellen DeGeneres Show")*.[[203]](#cite_note-ellenlive-203)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Christina_Aguilera_at_the_Breakthrough_Prize_Scientists%2C_2014.jpg/250px-Christina_Aguilera_at_the_Breakthrough_Prize_Scientists%2C_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christina_Aguilera_at_the_Breakthrough_Prize_Scientists,_2014.jpg)

Aguilera at the [Breakthrough Prize](/wiki/Breakthrough_Prize "Breakthrough Prize") Award ceremony in 2014

On February 22, 2013, Aguilera featured on rapper [Pitbull](/wiki/Pitbull_(rapper) "Pitbull (rapper)")'s "[Feel This Moment](/wiki/Feel_This_Moment "Feel This Moment")", which became the first of various international top-ten singles she would achieve that year, peaking at number eight on the *Billboard* Hot 100 becoming Aguilera's tenth top-ten song, and was later certified platinum by the RIAA.[[44]](#cite_note-RIAA-44)[[69]](#cite_note-Hot100-69)[[204]](#cite_note-hot1002-204) The song garnered positive reviews and was performed at the [2012 American Music Awards](/wiki/American_Music_Awards_of_2012 "American Music Awards of 2012"), [2013 Kids' Choice Awards](/wiki/2013_Kids%27_Choice_Awards "2013 Kids' Choice Awards"), *The Voice* and [2013 Billboard Music Awards](/wiki/2013_Billboard_Music_Awards "2013 Billboard Music Awards").[[205]](#cite_note-205)[[206]](#cite_note-206) In May 2013, Aguilera appeared on [Alejandro Fernández](/wiki/Alejandro_Fern%C3%A1ndez "Alejandro Fernández")'s cover of [Miguel Gallardo](/wiki/Miguel_Gallardo_(singer) "Miguel Gallardo (singer)")'s 1976 "[Hoy Tengo Ganas de Ti](/wiki/Hoy_Tengo_Ganas_de_Ti#Alejandro_Fernández_and_Christina_Aguilera_version "Hoy Tengo Ganas de Ti")" which received critical acclaim and earned them a diamond certification in Mexico.[[207]](#cite_note-207)

On November 4, 2013, Aguilera featured on a duet version of "[Say Something](/wiki/Say_Something_(A_Great_Big_World_song) "Say Something (A Great Big World song)")" with [A Great Big World](/wiki/A_Great_Big_World "A Great Big World").[[208]](#cite_note-artistdirect-208) The song became an instant success receiving universal acclaim from critics who praised Aguilera's stripped back vocals.[[209]](#cite_note-idolator-209) The song became Aguilera's second top-tenth hit on the *Billboard* Hot 100 peaking at number 4 and also became the second time she achieved multiple top-ten songs in a calendar year since 2000.[[210]](#cite_note-billboardtopten-210) They performed the song live on the [fifth season](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_season_5) "The Voice (American season 5)") of *The Voice* and [2013 American Music Awards](/wiki/2013_American_Music_Awards "2013 American Music Awards").[[211]](#cite_note-winners-211)[[212]](#cite_note-yahooII-212) The song earned them various awards including a six-time platinum certification from the RIAA[[44]](#cite_note-RIAA-44) and won [Best Pop Duo/Group Performance](/wiki/Best_Pop_Duo/Group_Performance "Best Pop Duo/Group Performance") at the [57th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/57th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "57th Annual Grammy Awards").[[213]](#cite_note-213)[[214]](#cite_note-214)[[215]](#cite_note-215)[[216]](#cite_note-216) In October of that year, Aguilera also lent her vocals to the [soundtrack](/wiki/The_Hunger_Games:_Catching_Fire_%E2%80%93_Original_Motion_Picture_Soundtrack "The Hunger Games: Catching Fire – Original Motion Picture Soundtrack") for *[The Hunger Games: Catching Fire](/wiki/The_Hunger_Games:_Catching_Fire "The Hunger Games: Catching Fire")* with the song "[We Remain](/wiki/We_Remain "We Remain")".[[217]](#cite_note-huff-217) She performed the song live with her contestant [Jacquie Lee](/wiki/Jacquie_Lee "Jacquie Lee") on *The Voice*.[[218]](#cite_note-consequenceofsound-218)

On January 1, 2014, Aguilera featured on the remix for "[Do What U Want](/wiki/Do_What_U_Want "Do What U Want")" by [Lady Gaga](/wiki/Lady_Gaga "Lady Gaga") to replace the original version with [R. Kelly](/wiki/R._Kelly "R. Kelly") after his sexual abuse allegations.[[219]](#cite_note-bbckelly-219)[[220]](#cite_note-Time-220) The two performed the song on the [fifth season](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_TV_series)_season_5 "The Voice (American TV series) season 5") of *[The Voice](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_TV_series) "The Voice (American TV series)")*.[[221]](#cite_note-221) In May, she performed a medley at both the [New Orleans Jazz & Heritage Festival](/wiki/New_Orleans_Jazz_%26_Heritage_Festival "New Orleans Jazz & Heritage Festival") and the 2014 KIIS FM [Wango Tango](/wiki/Wango_Tango "Wango Tango") concert.[[222]](#cite_note-222)[[223]](#cite_note-223) In November, Aguilera performed at the [Breakthrough Prize](/wiki/Breakthrough_Prize "Breakthrough Prize") Award ceremony.[[224]](#cite_note-224)

In February 2015, Aguilera opened the [2015 NBA All-Star Game](/wiki/2015_NBA_All-Star_Game "2015 NBA All-Star Game") alongside [The Rockettes](/wiki/The_Rockettes "The Rockettes") and [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas") with a [New York](/wiki/New_York,_New_York "New York, New York")-themed medley.[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[226]](#cite_note-226) In April, Aguilera starred in the [third season](/wiki/Nashville_(season_3) "Nashville (season 3)") of the musical drama series *[Nashville](/wiki/Nashville_(2012_TV_series) "Nashville (2012 TV series)")*, which aired on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company").[[227]](#cite_note-227) She also contributed to the series' soundtrack with "The Real Thing" and "[Shotgun](/wiki/Shotgun_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Shotgun (Christina Aguilera song)")" — with the latter reaching number twenty-eight on [Hot Country Songs](/wiki/Hot_Country_Songs "Hot Country Songs") chart.[[228]](#cite_note-228)[[229]](#cite_note-229) She also performed at the [50th Academy of Country Music Awards](/wiki/50th_Academy_of_Country_Music_Awards "50th Academy of Country Music Awards") alongside the [Rascal Flatts](/wiki/Rascal_Flatts "Rascal Flatts").[[230]](#cite_note-230)

### 2016–2020: *Liberation* and The Xperience

In May 2016, Aguilera exited *The Voice* following its [tenth](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_season_10) "The Voice (American season 10)") season, which she won with her contestant [Alisan Porter](/wiki/Alisan_Porter "Alisan Porter").[[231]](#cite_note-231) On the season finale, she performed "[I Have Nothing](/wiki/I_Have_Nothing "I Have Nothing")" and "[Dangerous Woman](/wiki/Dangerous_Woman_(song) "Dangerous Woman (song)")"; the latter with [Ariana Grande](/wiki/Ariana_Grande "Ariana Grande").[[232]](#cite_note-232) A month later, Aguilera released the song "[Change](/wiki/Change_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Change (Christina Aguilera song)")", in which she dedicated to the victims of the 2016 [Orlando nightclub shooting](/wiki/Orlando_nightclub_shooting "Orlando nightclub shooting").[[233]](#cite_note-233) The ballad received positive reviews and debuted at number five on *Billboard*'s [Bubbling Under Hot 100](/wiki/Bubbling_Under_Hot_100 "Bubbling Under Hot 100") chart.[[234]](#cite_note-234) In August 2016, Aguilera recorded a [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco") song titled "[Telepathy](/wiki/Telepathy_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Telepathy (Christina Aguilera song)")" featuring [Nile Rodgers](/wiki/Nile_Rodgers "Nile Rodgers") for the soundtrack of a [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") original series, *[The Get Down](/wiki/The_Get_Down "The Get Down")* (2016).[[235]](#cite_note-235) Her vocals were praised by critics and the song reached number one on the Dance Club Songs chart.[[236]](#cite_note-US_Dance-236)

In January 2017, Aguilera performed "[Stormy Weather](/wiki/Stormy_Weather_(song) "Stormy Weather (song)")" on the [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") television special *[Taking the Stage: African American Music and Stories That Changed America](/wiki/Taking_the_Stage:_African_American_Music_and_Stories_That_Changed_America "Taking the Stage: African American Music and Stories That Changed America")*.[[237]](#cite_note-237) In July, she voiced a video-game dancer in *[The Emoji Movie](/wiki/The_Emoji_Movie "The Emoji Movie")* (2017), with the film also featuring her song "Feel This Moment".[[238]](#cite_note-238)[[239]](#cite_note-239)[[240]](#cite_note-240) She also lent her vocals to the *[Served Like a Girl](/wiki/Served_Like_a_Girl "Served Like a Girl")* [documentary film](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film") for a song titled "America".[[241]](#cite_note-241) In November, Aguilera performed a tribute to [Whitney Houston](/wiki/Whitney_Houston "Whitney Houston") at the [2017 American Music Awards](/wiki/2017_American_Music_Awards "2017 American Music Awards").[[242]](#cite_note-242) The tribute received polarized views from fans and critics who praised her vocals but criticized why she was selected over a black artist.[[243]](#cite_note-243)[[244]](#cite_note-244)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/CAguilera_DenverOct2018_3.jpg/250px-CAguilera_DenverOct2018_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:CAguilera_DenverOct2018_3.jpg)

Aguilera performing on [the Liberation Tour](/wiki/The_Liberation_Tour_(Christina_Aguilera_tour) "The Liberation Tour (Christina Aguilera tour)") at the [Pepsi Center](/wiki/Pepsi_Center "Pepsi Center") in Denver, Colorado, 2018

In March 2018, Aguilera announced the completion of her then-upcoming eighth studio album alongside a *[Paper](/wiki/Paper_(magazine) "Paper (magazine)")* Magazine cover which received media coverage.[[245]](#cite_note-245) Aguilera released her eighth studio album *[Liberation](/wiki/Liberation_(Christina_Aguilera_album) "Liberation (Christina Aguilera album)")* on June 15, 2018, to favorable reviews.[[246]](#cite_note-246)[[247]](#cite_note-247)[[248]](#cite_note-Exults-248) The album had been in the works since the summer of 2015 and incorporated [R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B") and [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") elements.[[249]](#cite_note-249)[[250]](#cite_note-250)[[251]](#cite_note-Freedom-251) *Liberation* debuted at number six on the *Billboard* 200 chart, becoming Aguilera's seventh US top-ten album.[[252]](#cite_note-252) The album was promoted with multiple singles,[[253]](#cite_note-253)[[254]](#cite_note-254)[[255]](#cite_note-255)[[256]](#cite_note-256) including the preceding lead single, "[Accelerate](/wiki/Accelerate_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Accelerate (Christina Aguilera song)")" featuring [Ty Dolla Sign](/wiki/Ty_Dolla_Sign "Ty Dolla Sign") and [2 Chainz](/wiki/2_Chainz "2 Chainz"), which became Aguilera's tenth number one song on the US *Billboard* Dance Songs Chart.[[257]](#cite_note-257)[[258]](#cite_note-lvgs-258) Two other singles from the album, "[Fall in Line](/wiki/Fall_in_Line "Fall in Line")" (with [Demi Lovato](/wiki/Demi_Lovato "Demi Lovato")) and "[Like I Do](/wiki/Like_I_Do_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Like I Do (Christina Aguilera song)")", were nominated at the [61st Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/61st_Annual_Grammy_Awards "61st Annual Grammy Awards") for [Best Pop Duo/Group Performance](/wiki/Best_Pop_Duo/Group_Performance "Best Pop Duo/Group Performance") and [Best Rap/Sung Performance](/wiki/Best_Rap/Sung_Performance "Best Rap/Sung Performance") respectively.[[259]](#cite_note-259)

To further promote *Liberation*, Aguilera embarked on her first tour in 10 years, [The Liberation Tour](/wiki/The_Liberation_Tour_(Christina_Aguilera_tour) "The Liberation Tour (Christina Aguilera tour)"), which ran from September to November 2018 in the US.[[260]](#cite_note-260) The tour received positive reviews from critics who praised Aguilera's vocals and stage presence.[[261]](#cite_note-261) *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* named the tour one of the best 2018 live shows, and it was ranked at 132 on *[Pollstar](/wiki/Pollstar "Pollstar")*'s 2018 Year-End Top 200 North American Tours chart with a total gross of $8.7 million with an attendance of 77,854.[[262]](#cite_note-262)[[263]](#cite_note-billb-263) She also starred in the romantic science fiction *[Zoe](/wiki/Zoe_(2018_film) "Zoe (2018 film)")*, which premiered at the [Tribeca Film festival](/wiki/Tribeca_Film_festival "Tribeca Film festival") and was released in July of that year.[[264]](#cite_note-264)[[265]](#cite_note-265) At the end of 2018, Aguilera performed a medley of her songs on *[Dick Clark's New Year's Rockin' Eve](/wiki/Dick_Clark%27s_New_Year%27s_Rockin%27_Eve "Dick Clark's New Year's Rockin' Eve")*.[[266]](#cite_note-266)

In May 2019, Aguilera began headlining her first concert residency, [Christina Aguilera: The Xperience](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera:_The_Xperience "Christina Aguilera: The Xperience"),[[267]](#cite_note-267)[[268]](#cite_note-268)[[269]](#cite_note-269)[[270]](#cite_note-270)[[271]](#cite_note-271) a 25-date show at the [Zappos Theater](/wiki/Zappos_Theater "Zappos Theater") at [Planet Hollywood Las Vegas](/wiki/Planet_Hollywood_Las_Vegas "Planet Hollywood Las Vegas") which concluded in March 2020.[[272]](#cite_note-272) In between the residency, from July to December 2019, Aguilera also embarked on [The X Tour](/wiki/The_X_Tour_(Christina_Aguilera) "The X Tour (Christina Aguilera)"), which served as the international counterpart to The Liberation Tour, running throughout Europe and Latin America.[[273]](#cite_note-273)

In October 2019, Aguilera released the song ["Haunted Heart"](/wiki/Haunted_Heart_(Christina_Aguilera) "Haunted Heart (Christina Aguilera)") from the soundtrack of the animated film, *[The Addams Family](/wiki/The_Addams_Family_(2019_film) "The Addams Family (2019 film)")*,[[274]](#cite_note-274)[[275]](#cite_note-275)[[276]](#cite_note-276) and her second collaboration with [A Great Big World](/wiki/A_Great_Big_World "A Great Big World"), "[Fall on Me](/wiki/Fall_on_Me_(Andrea_Bocelli_and_Matteo_Bocelli_song)#A_Great_Big_World_and_Christina_Aguilera_version "Fall on Me (Andrea Bocelli and Matteo Bocelli song)")", followed a month later.[[277]](#cite_note-277) The former received critical acclaim from critics, was nominated for [Best Original Song in an Animated Film](/wiki/Hollywood_Music_in_Media_Award_for_Best_Original_Song_in_an_Animated_Film "Hollywood Music in Media Award for Best Original Song in an Animated Film") at the [Hollywood Music in Media Awards](/wiki/Hollywood_Music_in_Media_Awards "Hollywood Music in Media Awards") and topped the US [Kid Digital Song Sales](/wiki/Billboard_charts#Discontinued_chart "Billboard charts") chart.[[278]](#cite_note-hmima-278)

In early 2020, Aguilera recorded two songs for Disney's [live action remake of *Mulan*](/wiki/Mulan_(2020_film) "Mulan (2020 film)") (2020): "[Loyal Brave True](/wiki/Loyal_Brave_True "Loyal Brave True")" – released in March 2020 as a promotional single to positive reviews, eventually being shortlisted as one of the fifteen potential nominees for the [Academy Award for Best Original Song](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Song "Academy Award for Best Original Song")[[279]](#cite_note-279)[[280]](#cite_note-osc-280)[[281]](#cite_note-281) – and a re-recording of "[Reflection](/wiki/Reflection_(song)#2020_version "Reflection (song)")", released in August.[[282]](#cite_note-282) That year, she appeared on the ABC television special, *[The Disney Family Singalong](/wiki/The_Disney_Family_Singalong "The Disney Family Singalong")* and its [follow-up](/wiki/The_Disney_Family_Singalong#Volume_II "The Disney Family Singalong"), where she performed "[Can You Feel the Love Tonight](/wiki/Can_You_Feel_the_Love_Tonight "Can You Feel the Love Tonight")" and "[Remember Me](/wiki/Remember_Me_(Coco_song) "Remember Me (Coco song)")" respectively.[[283]](#cite_note-283) In November of that year, Aguilera signed a new management deal with [Roc Nation](/wiki/Roc_Nation "Roc Nation").[[284]](#cite_note-284)[[285]](#cite_note-285)

### 2021–present: *Aguilera* and Voltaire residency

In July 2021, Aguilera performed two sold-out shows at the [Hollywood Bowl](/wiki/Hollywood_Bowl "Hollywood Bowl") with [Gustavo Dudamel](/wiki/Gustavo_Dudamel "Gustavo Dudamel") and the [Los Angeles Philharmonic](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Philharmonic "Los Angeles Philharmonic").[[286]](#cite_note-hollbowl-286)[[287]](#cite_note-noise-287) She also recorded "[*The Addams Family* Theme](/wiki/The_Addams_Family_Theme "The Addams Family Theme")", which was featured on *[The Addams Family 2](/wiki/The_Addams_Family_2 "The Addams Family 2")* soundtrack.[[288]](#cite_note-288) In October, she performed "[Reflection](/wiki/Reflection_(song)#2020_version "Reflection (song)")", "[When You Wish Upon a Star](/wiki/When_You_Wish_Upon_a_Star "When You Wish Upon a Star")" and "[Loyal Brave True](/wiki/Loyal_Brave_True "Loyal Brave True")" for [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company")'s Walt Disney World's 50th Anniversary special.[[289]](#cite_note-289)[[290]](#cite_note-290)[[291]](#cite_note-291) On December 7, 2021, Aguilera was honored with the [People's Music Icon](/wiki/People%27s_Choice_Awards#People's_Music_Icon "People's Choice Awards") honorific award at the [47th People's Choice Awards](/wiki/47th_People%27s_Choice_Awards "47th People's Choice Awards").[[292]](#cite_note-292)

Aguilera released her ninth studio album, titled *[Aguilera](/wiki/Aguilera_(album) "Aguilera (album)")*, originally as a [double album](/wiki/Double_album "Double album") on May 31, 2022.[[293]](#cite_note-293)[[294]](#cite_note-294) The album paid tribute to different genres of [Latin music](/wiki/Latin_music "Latin music"),[[295]](#cite_note-295) and consisted of three separately released parts: *[La Fuerza](/wiki/La_Fuerza "La Fuerza")*,[[296]](#cite_note-billboard123-296) *[La Tormenta](/wiki/La_Tormenta_(EP) "La Tormenta (EP)")*,[[297]](#cite_note-297) and *[La Luz](/wiki/La_Luz_(EP) "La Luz (EP)")*.[[298]](#cite_note-298) The full album was released in its complete form on September 30, 2022.[[299]](#cite_note-299) The album was preceded with the singles "[Pa Mis Muchachas](/wiki/Pa_Mis_Muchachas "Pa Mis Muchachas")", "[Somos Nada](/wiki/Somos_Nada "Somos Nada")", "[Santo](/wiki/Santo_(song) "Santo (song)")" and followed with "[Suéltame](/wiki/Su%C3%A9ltame "Suéltame")" and "[No Es Que Te Extrañe](/wiki/No_Es_Que_Te_Extra%C3%B1e "No Es Que Te Extrañe")".[[300]](#cite_note-houstchron-300) *Aguilera* received widespread critical acclaim from music critics upon release, and was placed on several year-end lists by publications such as *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* and the *[Houston Chronicle](/wiki/Houston_Chronicle "Houston Chronicle")*.[[301]](#cite_note-billboardlist2022-301) The album was promoted through the [EU / UK Summer Series](/wiki/EU_/_UK_Summer_Series "EU / UK Summer Series") promotional tour, which consisted of five festival shows throughout Europe and three arena concerts in the United Kingdom.[[302]](#cite_note-summer-302)[[303]](#cite_note-303)[[304]](#cite_note-304) The promotional tour received critical praise.[[305]](#cite_note-Simpson-305)[[306]](#cite_note-Darcy-306)[[307]](#cite_note-307)[[308]](#cite_note-308)[[309]](#cite_note-309)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Christina_Aguilera_en_Marbella_2022_05_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Christina_Aguilera_en_Marbella_2022_05_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christina_Aguilera_en_Marbella_2022_05_(cropped).jpg)

Aguilera performing at the 2022 [Starlite Festival](/wiki/Starlite_Festival "Starlite Festival") held in [Marbella](/wiki/Marbella "Marbella")

*Aguilera* and its songs received seven nominations at the [23rd Annual Latin Grammy Awards](/wiki/23rd_Annual_Latin_Grammy_Awards "23rd Annual Latin Grammy Awards") with the album winning for [Best Traditional Pop Vocal Album](/wiki/Latin_Grammy_Award_for_Best_Traditional_Pop_Vocal_Album "Latin Grammy Award for Best Traditional Pop Vocal Album").[[310]](#cite_note-310)[[311]](#cite_note-311) The album also received another two nominations for [Best Latin Pop Album](/wiki/Best_Latin_Pop_Album "Best Latin Pop Album") and [Best Immersive Audio Album](/wiki/Best_Immersive_Audio_Album "Best Immersive Audio Album") at the [65th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/65th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "65th Annual Grammy Awards").[[312]](#cite_note-312) "No Es Que Te Extrañe" was also nominated for [Record of the Year](/wiki/Latin_Grammy_Award_for_Record_of_the_Year "Latin Grammy Award for Record of the Year") at the [24th Annual Latin Grammy Awards](/wiki/24th_Annual_Latin_Grammy_Awards "24th Annual Latin Grammy Awards").[[313]](#cite_note-313)

On June 11, 2022, Aguilera headlined the [Los Angeles Pride](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Pride "Los Angeles Pride") festival with guests [Mýa](/wiki/M%C3%BDa "Mýa"), [Kim Petras](/wiki/Kim_Petras "Kim Petras") and [Paris Hilton](/wiki/Paris_Hilton "Paris Hilton").[[314]](#cite_note-314) The use of a [strap-on dildo](/wiki/Strap-on_dildo "Strap-on dildo") during the set was the subject of polarized reception.[[315]](#cite_note-315)[[316]](#cite_note-316)[[317]](#cite_note-317) In October 2022, *Stripped* was reissued for its 20-year anniversary, featuring "I Will Be" (the b-side to "Dirrty") and [Benny Benassi](/wiki/Benny_Benassi "Benny Benassi")'s remix of "Beautiful".[[318]](#cite_note-318) A new music video for "Beautiful" was also released.[[319]](#cite_note-319)[[91]](#cite_note-Legacy-91) On November 10, 2022, [Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)") Studios announced a partnership with [Roc Nation](/wiki/Roc_Nation "Roc Nation") to produce an upcoming documentary about Aguilera.[[320]](#cite_note-320)[[321]](#cite_note-321)

On September 22, 2023, Aguilera released "[Learning to Fly](/wiki/Learning_to_Fly_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Learning to Fly (Christina Aguilera song)")" as a part of the soundtrack for *[PAW Patrol: The Mighty Movie](/wiki/PAW_Patrol:_The_Mighty_Movie "PAW Patrol: The Mighty Movie")*.[[322]](#cite_note-322) The same month she performed at the [EuroPride](/wiki/EuroPride "EuroPride") concert in [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta").[[323]](#cite_note-323)[[324]](#cite_note-324) At the end of the month, it was reported that Aguilera would executive produce [a stage adaptation of *Burlesque*](/wiki/Burlesque_(stage_musical) "Burlesque (stage musical)") which eventually made its debut in 2024, and its [West End](/wiki/West_End_theatre "West End theatre") debut in July 2025.[[325]](#cite_note-325)[[326]](#cite_note-326) In December 2023, Aguilera began her second residency, [Christina Aguilera at Voltaire](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera_at_Voltaire "Christina Aguilera at Voltaire"), at [The Venetian Las Vegas](/wiki/The_Venetian_Las_Vegas "The Venetian Las Vegas") which ran until August 2024.[[327]](#cite_note-327)[[328]](#cite_note-Christina_Aguilera_2023_y122-328)[[329]](#cite_note-329) In May 2024, it was reported that Aguilera had parted ways with [RCA Records](/wiki/RCA_Records "RCA Records") and signed with [5020 Records](/wiki/5020_Records "5020 Records"), a division of [Sony Music Latin](/wiki/Sony_Music_Latin "Sony Music Latin").[[330]](#cite_note-330)

In September 2024, Aguilera announced a "[Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") Anniversaries" performance for the 25th anniversary of her debut album.[[331]](#cite_note-331) The performances were released on both [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") as a recording, and Spotify as a [live extended play](/wiki/The_25th_Anniversary_of_Christina_Aguilera_(Spotify_Anniversaries_Live) "The 25th Anniversary of Christina Aguilera (Spotify Anniversaries Live)") on September 23,[[332]](#cite_note-332) while featuring appearances from [Machine Gun Kelly](/wiki/Machine_Gun_Kelly_(rapper) "Machine Gun Kelly (rapper)") and [Sabrina Carpenter](/wiki/Sabrina_Carpenter "Sabrina Carpenter").[[333]](#cite_note-Afnitos-333) In December, the EP was named one of the best albums of the year by *[HuffPost](/wiki/HuffPost "HuffPost")*.[[334]](#cite_note-334) In 2025, Aguilera headlined the September 2025 [Portola Music Festival](/wiki/Portola_Music_Festival "Portola Music Festival").[[335]](#cite_note-335)[[336]](#cite_note-336) A month later, *[Deadline Hollywood](/wiki/Deadline_Hollywood "Deadline Hollywood")* reported that she would star in a concert film, *[Christina Aguilera: Christmas in Paris](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera:_Christmas_in_Paris "Christina Aguilera: Christmas in Paris")*, directed by [Sam Wrench](/wiki/Sam_Wrench "Sam Wrench"), in support of the 25th anniversary of her Christmas album.[[337]](#cite_note-337) The film was released theatrically on December 14 and 21,[[338]](#cite_note-338) alongside a [live album](/wiki/Christmas_in_Paris#Soundtrack "Christmas in Paris") of the performances.[[339]](#cite_note-339)

## Artistry

### Influences

[![Etta James](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Etta_James.jpg/250px-Etta_James.jpg)](/wiki/File:Etta_James.jpg)

[![Mariah Carey](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Mariah_Carey13_Edwards_Dec_1998.jpg/250px-Mariah_Carey13_Edwards_Dec_1998.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mariah_Carey13_Edwards_Dec_1998.jpg)
