[![Whitney Houston](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Whitney_Houston_Welcome_Home_Heroes_1_cropped.jpg/250px-Whitney_Houston_Welcome_Home_Heroes_1_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Whitney_Houston_Welcome_Home_Heroes_1_cropped.jpg)

Musicians such as [Etta James](/wiki/Etta_James "Etta James") (left), [Mariah Carey](/wiki/Mariah_Carey "Mariah Carey") (center), and [Whitney Houston](/wiki/Whitney_Houston "Whitney Houston") (right) were cited among Aguilera's main influences. She later recalled shaping her singing voice after them in her early years.[[340]](#cite_note-340)

Aguilera has named [Etta James](/wiki/Etta_James "Etta James") as a "role model", and said "[At Last](/wiki/At_Last "At Last")" made a major impression on her.[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTER._Mead201229-341) She later stated, "I'll still be as raunchy as I wanna be, and I'll have [Etta]'s memory to back me up. She's what I want to be someday".[[342]](#cite_note-342) As a young girl, *[The Sound of Music](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music "The Sound of Music")* (1959) and its lead actress, [Julie Andrews](/wiki/Julie_Andrews "Julie Andrews"), became her early references to sing and perform, in addition to broadening her interest on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theater "Broadway theater").[[343]](#cite_note-343) American singers [Whitney Houston](/wiki/Whitney_Houston "Whitney Houston") and [Mariah Carey](/wiki/Mariah_Carey "Mariah Carey") were cited as Aguilera's "most apparent influences" having been compared to them throughout her career.[[344]](#cite_note-Behind-344) Aguilera's other major influences include [Billie Holiday](/wiki/Billie_Holiday "Billie Holiday"), and [Ella Fitzgerald](/wiki/Ella_Fitzgerald "Ella Fitzgerald").[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenberger200827-345) She has also cited rock bands as an influence, specifically the [Red Hot Chili Peppers](/wiki/Red_Hot_Chili_Peppers "Red Hot Chili Peppers"), [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)"), and [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead").[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez200340-346)[[347]](#cite_note-347)

Aguilera was also influenced by Latin music, recalling that during her childhood: "I grew up hearing [Spanish] being spoken in my household [and] hearing a lot of [Julio Iglesias](/wiki/Julio_Iglesias "Julio Iglesias") on the record player".[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominguez20033-348) Some of her other inspirations in Latin music include [Chavela Vargas](/wiki/Chavela_Vargas "Chavela Vargas"), [Vicente Fernández](/wiki/Vicente_Fern%C3%A1ndez "Vicente Fernández"), and younger artists such [Rosalía](/wiki/Rosal%C3%ADa "Rosalía").[[349]](#cite_note-349)[[350]](#cite_note-350) As a performer, Aguilera credits [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna") and [Janet Jackson](/wiki/Janet_Jackson "Janet Jackson") as major inspirations for being "positive female artists [who] aren't afraid to take chances, be daring, experimental and sexy".[[351]](#cite_note-351) [Cher](/wiki/Cher "Cher") also encouraged her to be a performer and "a woman who has the guts to do [everything]".[[352]](#cite_note-352)

Aguilera's childhood visits to records stores molded her taste and led to an appreciation for blues and soul music, which she called "music that really had heart".[[353]](#cite_note-353) Her music has also paid homage to many artists from this genre, including [Aretha Franklin](/wiki/Aretha_Franklin "Aretha Franklin"), [Marvin Gaye](/wiki/Marvin_Gaye "Marvin Gaye"), [Nina Simone](/wiki/Nina_Simone "Nina Simone"), and [Otis Redding](/wiki/Otis_Redding "Otis Redding"). Her album *Back to Basics* (2006) features inspirations of musicians from the early 20th century. As she later commented: "I was just so drawn to that [kind of music] ... [It] has so much pain, so much beauty of raw emotion and passion".[[13]](#cite_note-OldSoul-13) Her other influences in the genre was added for *Liberation* (2018), including [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson") in "Maria" and [Janis Joplin](/wiki/Janis_Joplin "Janis Joplin") in "Sick of Sittin'".[[354]](#cite_note-354)[[248]](#cite_note-Exults-248)

Outside of the music industry, Aguilera was inspired by actresses from the [Old Hollywood](/wiki/Old_Hollywood "Old Hollywood").[[355]](#cite_note-355) She later stated, "I'm fascinated with the golden age of film. I wanted to capture the authenticity of the glam era", after which she cited examples such as [Marlene Dietrich](/wiki/Marlene_Dietrich "Marlene Dietrich"), [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe"), [Greta Garbo](/wiki/Greta_Garbo "Greta Garbo"), [Veronica Lake](/wiki/Veronica_Lake "Veronica Lake"), and the [pin-up models](/wiki/Pin-up_model "Pin-up model").[[356]](#cite_note-356) Her "Tilt Ya Head Back" music video also recreated Monroe's signature look, while the video for "Your Body" includes a reference to [Lucille Ball](/wiki/Lucille_Ball "Lucille Ball").[[357]](#cite_note-357)[[358]](#cite_note-358) The video for "Ain't No Other Man" presented Aguilera under the [alter ego](/wiki/Alter_ego "Alter ego") Baby Jane, which was inspired by [Bette Davis](/wiki/Bette_Davis "Bette Davis")'s character in psychological horror film *[What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?](/wiki/What_Ever_Happened_to_Baby_Jane%3F_(film) "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane? (film)")* (1962).[[359]](#cite_note-Blues-359) Her other inspirations mostly came from art works, including those by [Andy Warhol](/wiki/Andy_Warhol "Andy Warhol"), [Roy Lichtenstein](/wiki/Roy_Lichtenstein "Roy Lichtenstein") and [Banksy](/wiki/Banksy "Banksy").[[360]](#cite_note-360)[[361]](#cite_note-361)

### Voice

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/What_A_Girl_Wants122_-_CA.jpg/250px-What_A_Girl_Wants122_-_CA.jpg)](/wiki/File:What_A_Girl_Wants122_-_CA.jpg)

Aguilera is known by her [melismatic singing](/wiki/Melisma "Melisma") technique, yielding comparisons to Whitney Houston and Mariah Carey.[[362]](#cite_note-ClassicFM-362)[[363]](#cite_note-Songbirds-363)

Critics have described Aguilera as a [soprano](/wiki/Soprano "Soprano"),[[14]](#cite_note-ChildStar-14)[[364]](#cite_note-364) possessing a four-octave [vocal range](/wiki/Vocal_range "Vocal range").[[365]](#cite_note-365) She is also able to perform the [whistle register](/wiki/Whistle_register "Whistle register").[[366]](#cite_note-366) [Maura Johnston](/wiki/Maura_Johnston "Maura Johnston"), a journalist with *[Slate](/wiki/Slate_(magazine) "Slate (magazine)")*, called Aguilera's voice "an instrument that despite its ability to leap octaves has a low-end grounding similar to that possessed by opera singers".[[367]](#cite_note-367) *[The Boston Globe](/wiki/The_Boston_Globe "The Boston Globe")* columnist Joan Anderman highlighted her vocal versatility: "[She] is a real singer ... blessed with the sort of breathtaking elasticity, golden tones, and sheer power that separate the divas from the dabblers".[[368]](#cite_note-368) In addition to sustain high notes, Aguilera is recognized for making use of the [melismatic](/wiki/Melisma "Melisma") technique.[[362]](#cite_note-ClassicFM-362) [Jon Pareles](/wiki/Jon_Pareles "Jon Pareles") from *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* emphasized that "she can aim a note as directly as a missile or turn its trajectory into an aerobatic spiral of leaping, quivering, and scalloping melismas".[[248]](#cite_note-Exults-248) [Ron Fair](/wiki/Ron_Fair "Ron Fair")—[A&R](/wiki/A%26R "A&R") executive from [RCA Records](/wiki/RCA_Records "RCA Records")—was impressed by her "perfect intonation", and concluded: "She's got the pipes to be the next [Barbra Streisand](/wiki/Barbra_Streisand "Barbra Streisand") or [Céline Dion](/wiki/C%C3%A9line_Dion "Céline Dion")".[[344]](#cite_note-Behind-344)

Aguilera's singing technique also yielded comparisons to other singers. Journalist [David Browne](/wiki/David_Browne_(journalist) "David Browne (journalist)") suggested that, alongside Mariah Carey and Whitney Houston, she "forms the team of the main proponents" of the melismatic technique.[[363]](#cite_note-Songbirds-363) Richard Harrington from *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* also noticed similarities with both artists: "She has a genuinely powerful voice that's evoked comparisons [to them], though it for the most part avoids those singers' ornamental mannerisms".[[369]](#cite_note-Sensation-369) *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")* columnist [Sasha Frere-Jones](/wiki/Sasha_Frere-Jones "Sasha Frere-Jones") also credited the technique by making her become "a serious singer without needing to reincarnate the [Sarah Vaughan](/wiki/Sarah_Vaughan "Sarah Vaughan")".[[370]](#cite_note-Symbols-370) Songwriter [Steve Kipner](/wiki/Steve_Kipner "Steve Kipner") praises Aguilera's vocal dexterity, including her ability to "internalize[d] all the riffs from [Chaka Khan](/wiki/Chaka_Khan "Chaka Khan")".[[344]](#cite_note-Behind-344) *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* music critic [Ann Powers](/wiki/Ann_Powers "Ann Powers") wrote that she possesses a "voice purely powerful as that of Etta James [...] and she's moving toward the expressiveness of [Gladys Knight](/wiki/Gladys_Knight "Gladys Knight"), if not Aretha Franklin". Powers also associated her vocal ability with [Donna Summer](/wiki/Donna_Summer "Donna Summer") when she performs records influenced by [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues").[[371]](#cite_note-371) *[Minneapolis Star Tribune](/wiki/Minneapolis_Star_Tribune "Minneapolis Star Tribune")* noted that "She has the best voice of the bunch — big, rich, soulful."[[372]](#cite_note-372)

However, Aguilera has also been criticized for the frequent use of melisma, as well as for her style of singing live during concerts.[[373]](#cite_note-373)[[128]](#cite_note-Greatness-128) *[HuffPost](/wiki/HuffPost "HuffPost")* columnist John Eskow called her "the main proponent of oversouling". Eskow praised Aguilera's voice as a "great instrument", though complained she did not "seem to know when to stop" with the use of "gratuitous and confected melisma".[[374]](#cite_note-374) Lucy Davies, music critic from [BBC Music](/wiki/BBC_Music "BBC Music"), raved about her "stunning voice", but offered that "she could be more varied, simply by cutting out some of the 'ye-e-eeeh, woah' in her songs".[[375]](#cite_note-375) Writing for *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")*, Chris Willman credits Aguilera's singing style as an influence of Mariah Carey, noting "her slightly nasal tone that really only becomes obvious when she's overselling a song".[[376]](#cite_note-376) Alexa Tietjen from [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1") acknowledged that Aguilera "does tend to take it to the extreme at times [...] but her vocal prowess is what's gotten her so far. Love them or hate them, the riffs are a part of who she is as a performer".[[377]](#cite_note-377)

### Musical style

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

["Genie in a Bottle" (1999)](/wiki/File:Genie_in_a_Bottle_(Christina_Aguilera_song_-_sample).ogg "File:Genie in a Bottle (Christina Aguilera song - sample).ogg")

"Genie in a Bottle", an uptempo [teen pop](/wiki/Teen_pop "Teen pop") song, whose genre also featured on Aguilera's early records.[[51]](#cite_note-Ranked-51)[[6]](#cite_note-Remade-6)

---

["Like I Do" (2018)](/wiki/File:Like_I_Do_-_Christina_Aguilera.ogg "File:Like I Do - Christina Aguilera.ogg")

A midtempo [alternative R&B](/wiki/Alternative_R%26B "Alternative R&B") song,[[378]](#cite_note-378) "Like I Do" showcases Aguilera's post-2000 works influenced by R&B and hip-hop.[[379]](#cite_note-Masterwork-379)

---

*Problems playing these files? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

Aguilera's discography is generally categorized as [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music"), [R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B"), [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop"), and [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music").[[379]](#cite_note-Masterwork-379)[[380]](#cite_note-Q&A-380) According to critics, she has also experimented with other musical genres in her works. In a 2008 interview, she explained that she is "always inspired by new things" in music and enjoys "experimenting with [her] voice".[[156]](#cite_note-Hits-156) Alexis Petridis from *The Guardian* considered the "boldness in [continually] reinventing" her music as "one of her most impressive facets" as an artist.[[381]](#cite_note-381) Initially established as a [bubblegum pop](/wiki/Bubblegum_pop "Bubblegum pop") singer, she mixed [teen pop](/wiki/Teen_pop "Teen pop") and [dance-pop](/wiki/Dance-pop "Dance-pop") on her first records, *Christina Aguilera* (1999) and *Mi Reflejo* (2000), with the latter also taking inspiration from numerous genres in [Latin music](/wiki/Latin_music "Latin music").[[369]](#cite_note-Sensation-369)[[382]](#cite_note-382)

Moving away from the teen niche, Aguilera's post-2000 material mainly incorporated R&B and hip-hop.[[379]](#cite_note-Masterwork-379)[[80]](#cite_note-Puppet-80) Listed as an [executive producer](/wiki/Executive_producer "Executive producer") on *Stripped* (2002), she mixed both genres with [Latin pop](/wiki/Latin_pop "Latin pop"), [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), [neo-soul](/wiki/Neo-soul "Neo-soul"), [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), among others, while *Back to Basics* (2006) was described as a "throwback with elements of old-school genres combined with a modern-day twist [and] hard-hitting beats".[[123]](#cite_note-Throwback-123) She continued merging R&B with other musical styles on *Burlesque* (2010), as well on *Liberation* (2018), which also features hip-hop, soul and blues elements.[[383]](#cite_note-383)[[384]](#cite_note-384)

The [show tune](/wiki/Show_tune "Show tune") "Lady Marmalade" is noted as Aguilera's first musical transformation, which RCA Records initially felt was "too urban".[[116]](#cite_note-Memories-116)[[385]](#cite_note-385) Despite the renewal in her repertoire, Aguilera continued producing [power ballads](/wiki/Power_ballad "Power ballad")—which became a signature in her discography—in between her uptempo material.[[386]](#cite_note-386) [Kelefa Sanneh](/wiki/Kelefa_Sanneh "Kelefa Sanneh"), music critic from *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, observed the continuous modification in her music, in addition to highlighting her "decision to snub some of the big-name producers on whom pop stars often rely".[[387]](#cite_note-Honey-387) Her sonically drastic ventures include the subversive "galactic pop" on *Bionic* (2010),[[162]](#cite_note-single11-162) [electropop](/wiki/Electropop "Electropop") club on *Lotus* (2012),[[388]](#cite_note-388) and Mexican [ranchera](/wiki/Ranchera "Ranchera") numbers on *Aguilera* (2022).[[389]](#cite_note-389)

## Cultural status

Main articles: [Cultural impact of Christina Aguilera](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Christina_Aguilera "Cultural impact of Christina Aguilera") and [Public image of Christina Aguilera](/wiki/Public_image_of_Christina_Aguilera "Public image of Christina Aguilera")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Christina_Aguilera_2019_Disney_Legends_Awards_Ceremony_D23_EXPO_2019_%2851440742003%29_%28cropped_2%29.jpg/250px-Christina_Aguilera_2019_Disney_Legends_Awards_Ceremony_D23_EXPO_2019_%2851440742003%29_%28cropped_2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christina_Aguilera_2019_Disney_Legends_Awards_Ceremony_D23_EXPO_2019_(51440742003)_(cropped_2).jpg)

Aguilera at the [Disney Legends](/wiki/Disney_Legends "Disney Legends") awards ceremony in 2019

Throughout her career, Aguilera has been called a [pop icon](/wiki/Pop_icon "Pop icon"),[[390]](#cite_note-390) a [gay icon](/wiki/Gay_icon "Gay icon"),[[391]](#cite_note-391)[[392]](#cite_note-392) a [triple threat entertainer](/wiki/Triple_threat_(entertainer) "Triple threat (entertainer)"),[[393]](#cite_note-393) and a [fashion icon](/wiki/Fashion_icon "Fashion icon").[[394]](#cite_note-394)[[395]](#cite_note-395) She has been ranked among the greatest singers of all time and referred to as the "[Voice of a Generation](/wiki/Honorific_nicknames_in_popular_music "Honorific nicknames in popular music")".[[396]](#cite_note-396)[[397]](#cite_note-397) She has been labeled a "[diva](/wiki/Diva "Diva")" for her stardom and persona,[[398]](#cite_note-BustleAguilera1-398) garnering polarized views, and various comparisons to [Mariah Carey](/wiki/Mariah_Carey "Mariah Carey").[[398]](#cite_note-BustleAguilera1-398) She has attracted criticism for wearing revealing clothes,[[399]](#cite_note-PostG-399) and has been called a [sex symbol](/wiki/Sex_symbol "Sex symbol").[[370]](#cite_note-Symbols-370)[[6]](#cite_note-Remade-6) Aguilera has also been noted for her close affiliation with [The Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company"), being honored as a [Disney Legend](/wiki/Disney_Legend "Disney Legend") in 2019.[[400]](#cite_note-DisneyL-400)

She has often received media attention for her reinventions.[[401]](#cite_note-401)[[402]](#cite_note-402) In the early 2000s, Aguilera rose to fame as a [teen idol](/wiki/Teen_idol "Teen idol") with her [bubblegum pop](/wiki/Bubblegum_pop "Bubblegum pop") [eponymous debut album](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera_(album) "Christina Aguilera (album)").[[403]](#cite_note-status1-403) Various images she took on included a provocative image following the release of her single "[Dirrty](/wiki/Dirrty "Dirrty")", an old Hollywood image, and a futuristic look.[[403]](#cite_note-status1-403) She was later ranked as one of the most successful artists of the 2000s decade by *Billboard*.[[404]](#cite_note-404) In the 2010s, Aguilera saw moderate success with her music while spending six seasons as a coach on *[The Voice](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_TV_series) "The Voice (American TV series)")*.[[185]](#cite_note-Jean-Philippe-185) Aguilera's personal struggles were often highlighted in the media.[[405]](#cite_note-media5-405) She saw a musical comeback with *[Liberation](/wiki/Liberation_(Christina_Aguilera_album) "Liberation (Christina Aguilera album)")* (2018).[[406]](#cite_note-406)

Throughout her career, Aguilera has faced media attention for her physical appearances with some journalists noting that she has often received [body shaming](/wiki/Body_shaming "Body shaming") critical comments.[[407]](#cite_note-407) She was named one of the artists who revived [teen pop](/wiki/Teen_pop "Teen pop") in the late 1990s.[[48]](#cite_note-Time2000-48) Aguilera has also been a significant figure in helping reshape the "Latin explosion" in the late 1990s and contributed to the [Latin pop boom in American music](/wiki/Latin_American_music_in_the_United_States#2000s_Latin_pop_boom "Latin American music in the United States") with her album *Mi Reflejo* (2000), which went on to become the [best-selling Latin pop album of all time](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_Latin_albums "List of best-selling Latin albums").[[56]](#cite_note-yearend2000-56)[[408]](#cite_note-legacy1-408) She has also been noted for honoring her [Ecuadorian](/wiki/Ecuadorian "Ecuadorian") roots throughout her career.[[409]](#cite_note-Forbeslatin1-409)

She has heavily incorporated controversial themes in her music.[[410]](#cite_note-Watershed-410) Various themes include [feminism](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism"), [sexuality](/wiki/Sexuality "Sexuality"), defending the [sex-positive movement](/wiki/Sex-positive_movement "Sex-positive movement"), [domestic violence](/wiki/Domestic_violence "Domestic violence"), among others.[[410]](#cite_note-Watershed-410) Gerrick D. Kennedy writing for the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* opined that Aguilera "tackled subject matters her contemporaries were shying away from" which made her stand out amongst her peers in the early 2000s.[[380]](#cite_note-Q&A-380) The impact of her [videography](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera_videography "Christina Aguilera videography"), particularly the music videos for "Dirrty" and "[Beautiful](/wiki/Beautiful_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Beautiful (Christina Aguilera song)")", has also been analyzed by music critics.[[411]](#cite_note-Jeri-411) Subsequently, Aguilera has influenced and inspired several acts and artists in industries worldwide.[[412]](#cite_note-412)

## Achievements

Main article: [List of awards and nominations received by Christina Aguilera](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Christina_Aguilera "List of awards and nominations received by Christina Aguilera")

Aguilera has accumulated numerous awards and honorary accolades throughout her career. At the age of 19, she won the [Grammy Award for Best New Artist](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_New_Artist "Grammy Award for Best New Artist"), becoming one of the youngest artists to receive the award.[[413]](#cite_note-413) Aguilera went on to receive four more Grammy Awards from [The Recording Academy](/wiki/The_Recording_Academy "The Recording Academy"), earning twenty nominations overall.[[414]](#cite_note-414) She is also a recipient of two [Latin Grammy Awards](/wiki/Latin_Grammy_Awards "Latin Grammy Awards"), three [*Billboard* Latin Music Awards](/wiki/Billboard_Latin_Music_Awards "Billboard Latin Music Awards")—including the [Spirit of Hope Award](/wiki/Billboard_Spirit_of_Hope_Award "Billboard Spirit of Hope Award")—, two [MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Awards "MTV Video Music Awards") (VMAs), two [People's Choice Awards](/wiki/People%27s_Choice_Awards "People's Choice Awards")—including the inaugural [Music Icon Award](/wiki/47th_People%27s_Choice_Awards#Other "47th People's Choice Awards")—, three [World Music Awards](/wiki/World_Music_Awards "World Music Awards"), and one [Guinness World Record](/wiki/Guinness_World_Record "Guinness World Record").[[415]](#cite_note-415)[[416]](#cite_note-RevivedY2K-416)[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFolkard2005360-417) Aguilera is also one of the [best-selling music artists of all time](/wiki/Best-selling_music_artists_of_all_time "Best-selling music artists of all time"), with estimated sales of over 100 million records sold worldwide.[[418]](#cite_note-418)[[419]](#cite_note-419)[[420]](#cite_note-420) Among other honors, she received a star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") in 2010 "recognition of her achievements in the [recording industry](/wiki/Recording_industry "Recording industry")".[[421]](#cite_note-421) *Billboard* ranked her at number 20 on its 2025 "Top 100 Women Artists of the 21st Century" list.[[422]](#cite_note-422)

## Other activities

### Business ventures and wealth

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Christina_Aguilera_%28at_premiere_of_%22To_John_With_Love%22%2C_September_2010%29.jpg/250px-Christina_Aguilera_%28at_premiere_of_%22To_John_With_Love%22%2C_September_2010%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christina_Aguilera_(at_premiere_of_%22To_John_With_Love%22,_September_2010).jpg)

Aguilera attending a fundraising event promoted by [Montblanc](/wiki/Montblanc_(company) "Montblanc (company)") in 2010

As a businesswoman, Aguilera has been selling her own products and investing in multiple companies. In 2008, she became co-owner of [LAX](/wiki/Luxor_Las_Vegas#LAX_nightclub "Luxor Las Vegas") nightclub at the [Luxor Las Vegas](/wiki/Luxor_Las_Vegas "Luxor Las Vegas") alongside [DJ AM](/wiki/DJ_AM "DJ AM").[[423]](#cite_note-423) In 2015, she founded the MX Productions & Investments in partnership with fiancée Matthew Rutler, for which they was included in the investors' group of [Pinterest](/wiki/Pinterest "Pinterest"), [DraftKings](/wiki/DraftKings "DraftKings"), [Lyft](/wiki/Lyft "Lyft") and [MasterClass](/wiki/MasterClass "MasterClass") enterprises.[[424]](#cite_note-MXCompany-424) Aguilera also joined the ownership group of [Angel City FC](/wiki/Angel_City_FC "Angel City FC"), a [National Women's Soccer League](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Soccer_League "National Women's Soccer League") (NWSL) expansion team, and [sexual wellness](/wiki/Sexual_and_reproductive_health "Sexual and reproductive health") brand [Playground](/wiki/Playground_(company) "Playground (company)").[[425]](#cite_note-AngelCity-425)[[426]](#cite_note-426) In March 2015, Aguilera and Rutler signed a deal with [Lions Gate Entertainment](/wiki/Lions_Gate_Entertainment "Lions Gate Entertainment") to developed both scripted and unscripted projects which eventually was acquired by [ABC Family](/wiki/Freeform_(TV_channel) "Freeform (TV channel)") and [Spike TV](/wiki/Spike_TV "Spike TV") networks.[[427]](#cite_note-427)[[428]](#cite_note-428) In 2022, she was named [chief culture officer](/wiki/Chief_culture_officer "Chief culture officer") of [The Fun Wine Company](/wiki/The_Fun_Wine_Company "The Fun Wine Company").[[429]](#cite_note-429)

In 2007, Aguilera signed a long-term deal with [Procter & Gamble](/wiki/Procter_%26_Gamble "Procter & Gamble") (P&G) and created the [Christina Aguilera Fragrances](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera_Fragrances "Christina Aguilera Fragrances"), which has been sold in North America, Latin America, Asia and Europe.[[430]](#cite_note-430)[[431]](#cite_note-431) In addition to scents, the brand manufactures [body lotions](/wiki/Body_lotion "Body lotion"), [body sprays](/wiki/Body_spray "Body spray"), and [deodorants](/wiki/Deodorant "Deodorant") whose products was ranked among the United Kingdom best-sellers in 2007, 2009 and 2010.[[432]](#cite_note-432) In January 2016, the line was reported to have sold over $80 million.[[433]](#cite_note-433) Her fragrances also received numerous accolades, including at the [Duftstars Awards](/wiki/The_Fragrance_Foundation "The Fragrance Foundation") and [FiFi Awards](/wiki/FiFi_Awards "FiFi Awards").[[434]](#cite_note-434)[[435]](#cite_note-435) During [São Paulo Fashion Week](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Fashion_Week "São Paulo Fashion Week") in 2011, Aguilera debuted her first clothes line which was mostly sold in Brazilian department stores [C&A](/wiki/C%26A "C&A").[[436]](#cite_note-436) In partnership with [American Greetings](/wiki/American_Greetings "American Greetings"), she featured in customized birthday video cards which was sold online in 2021 and eventually won a [Clio Awards](/wiki/Clio_Awards "Clio Awards").[[437]](#cite_note-437)

During her career, Aguilera has been involved in marketing initiatives and endorsed numerous brands including [Sears](/wiki/Sears "Sears"), [Levi's](/wiki/Levi%27s "Levi's"),[[438]](#cite_note-438) [Skechers](/wiki/Skechers "Skechers"),[[439]](#cite_note-439) [Mercedes-Benz](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz "Mercedes-Benz"),[[440]](#cite_note-440) [Virgin Mobile](/wiki/Virgin_Mobile "Virgin Mobile"),[[441]](#cite_note-441) [Orange UK](/wiki/Orange_UK "Orange UK"), [Sony Ericsson](/wiki/Sony_Ericsson "Sony Ericsson"),[[442]](#cite_note-442) [Oreo](/wiki/Oreo "Oreo"),[[443]](#cite_note-443) [SweeTarts](/wiki/SweeTarts "SweeTarts"),[[444]](#cite_note-444) [Just Eat](/wiki/Just_Eat "Just Eat"), [Lieferando](/wiki/Lieferando "Lieferando"), [Menulog](/wiki/Menulog "Menulog"),[[445]](#cite_note-445) [Merz Aesthetics](/wiki/Merz_Pharma#Merz_Aesthetics_GmbH "Merz Pharma"),[[446]](#cite_note-446) and [Grindr](/wiki/Grindr "Grindr").[[447]](#cite_note-447) In 2001, she starred in a series of television advertising to [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola"), which was aired in Latin America and the United States. Media outlets reported the deal signed by Aguilera to be worth up to £50 million.[[448]](#cite_note-448) Aguilera also shot a global scale advertising campaign alongside South Korean singer [Rain](/wiki/Rain_(entertainer) "Rain (entertainer)") to promote [Pepsi](/wiki/Pepsi "Pepsi") during the [2006 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup").[[449]](#cite_note-449) Other Aguilera's endorses included a clothing line by [Versace](/wiki/Versace "Versace") in 2003, and a collection of [sterling silver](/wiki/Sterling_silver "Sterling silver") pieces designed by [Stephen Webster](/wiki/Stephen_Webster "Stephen Webster") in 2008.[[450]](#cite_note-450)[[451]](#cite_note-451) On June 28, 2004, she received £200,000 to open the summer sale at the London's department store [Harrods](/wiki/Harrods "Harrods").[[452]](#cite_note-452) In 2008, Aguilera was paid $1.5 million to submit pictures of her first child to *[People](/wiki/People_(magazine) "People (magazine)")*, becoming the [ninth most expensive celebrity baby photograph ever taken](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_celebrity_photographs "List of most expensive celebrity photographs").[[453]](#cite_note-453)

In 2000, Aguilera was among the biggest moneymakers in the music business with over £65 million earned with album sales, merchandising and licensing deals according to [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1").[[454]](#cite_note-454) In January 2008, she was ranked 19th on *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*' list of Top-Earning Women in Music of 2007 with earnings of $20 million.[[455]](#cite_note-455) The previous year, she was also included on their list of richest women in entertainment industry, with an estimated net worth of $60 million.[[456]](#cite_note-456) In August 2022, Aguilera's net worth was reported to be $160 million.[[457]](#cite_note-457)

### Philanthropy and activism

Referred to as a humanitarian, Aguilera has done philanthropic works during her career.[[458]](#cite_note-458) In 2003, she visited the [Women's Center & Shelter of Greater Pittsburgh](/wiki/Women%27s_Center_%26_Shelter_of_Greater_Pittsburgh "Women's Center & Shelter of Greater Pittsburgh") and provided a donation over $200,000 in support for victims of [domestic violence](/wiki/Domestic_violence "Domestic violence").[[459]](#cite_note-459) In addition to starred in television advertisings on the [Lifetime](/wiki/Lifetime_(TV_network) "Lifetime (TV network)") network calling for the end of violence against women in 2007, she partnered with [Verizon](/wiki/Verizon "Verizon") in a campaign to awareness about violence domestic in 2015.[[460]](#cite_note-460)[[461]](#cite_note-461) She later destined proceeds from the [Christina Aguilera: The Xperience](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera:_The_Xperience "Christina Aguilera: The Xperience") (2019–2020) residency to [The Shade Tree](/wiki/The_Shade_Tree "The Shade Tree"), a women and children's center in [Las Vegas, Nevada](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada").[[462]](#cite_note-462) In October 2019, Aguilera efforts against domestic violence earned her the [Bonnie Polley Community Hero Award](/wiki/The_Shade_Tree#History "The Shade Tree").[[463]](#cite_note-463)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Secretary_Clinton_With_David_Novak_and_Christina_Aguilera_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Secretary_Clinton_With_David_Novak_and_Christina_Aguilera_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Secretary_Clinton_With_David_Novak_and_Christina_Aguilera_(cropped).jpg)

Aguilera in 2012 with [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") and [David Novak](/wiki/David_C._Novak "David C. Novak") at the [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme") USA Awards Ceremony in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

In 2009, Aguilera became the global spokesperson for the [World Food Program](/wiki/World_Food_Program "World Food Program"), a branch of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (UN).[[464]](#cite_note-464) She visited countries with high rates of malnutrions, including Ecuador, Guatemala, Haiti and Rwanda.[[465]](#cite_note-465) As of 2013, it was reported she helped raise over $148 million to the organization and other hunger relief agencies in 45 countries.[[466]](#cite_note-466) Aguilera's efforts eventually earned her the [Muhammad Ali Humanitarian Awards](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Center "Muhammad Ali Center") and [George McGovern Leadership Awards](/wiki/World_Hunger_Relief#History "World Hunger Relief")—which she received in the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") from the former [Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State"), [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton").[[467]](#cite_note-467)[[468]](#cite_note-468) Her other philanthropy initiatives included donations of her wedding gifts to charities in support of families affected by [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina") in 2005 and concerts to urges donations to the [American Red Cross](/wiki/American_Red_Cross "American Red Cross") due [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") in 2012.[[469]](#cite_note-469)[[470]](#cite_note-470)

In 2010, Aguilera auctioned tickets to her concerts through [Christie's](/wiki/Christie%27s "Christie's"), earmarking the proceeds to [non-profit](/wiki/Non-profit "Non-profit") [environmental organizations](/wiki/Environmental_organization "Environmental organization"), including [Conservation International](/wiki/Conservation_International "Conservation International") and the [Natural Resources Defense Council](/wiki/Natural_Resources_Defense_Council "Natural Resources Defense Council").[[471]](#cite_note-471) In response to the [2010 Haiti earthquake](/wiki/2010_Haiti_earthquake "2010 Haiti earthquake"), she auctioned off a [Chrysler 300](/wiki/Chrysler_300 "Chrysler 300") and destined proceeds to support disaster victims and their families.[[472]](#cite_note-472) Additionally, Aguilera appeared on the *[Hope for Haiti Now](/wiki/Hope_for_Haiti_Now "Hope for Haiti Now")* telethon, whose donations directly benefited [Oxfam America](/wiki/Oxfam_America "Oxfam America"), [Partners In Health](/wiki/Partners_In_Health "Partners In Health"), [International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement](/wiki/International_Red_Cross_and_Red_Crescent_Movement "International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement"), and [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF").[[473]](#cite_note-473)[[474]](#cite_note-474) She also was praised by [People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals](/wiki/People_for_the_Ethical_Treatment_of_Animals "People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals") (PETA) for signed an open letter destined to [Government of South Korea](/wiki/Government_of_South_Korea "Government of South Korea") appealing to the ban of consumption of dogs and cats, and for using fake fox fur in concerts costumes.[[475]](#cite_note-475)[[476]](#cite_note-476)

As a [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights "LGBT rights") activist, Aguilera was lauded for calling out myths around [HIV/AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS"), in addition to raising awareness about the virus and raising funds for the [Mac AIDS Fund](/wiki/Mac_AIDS_Fund "Mac AIDS Fund") and the [Elton John AIDS Foundation](/wiki/Elton_John_AIDS_Foundation "Elton John AIDS Foundation").[[477]](#cite_note-477)[[478]](#cite_note-478) In 2008, she advocated for [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") and spoke out against [Proposition 8](/wiki/Proposition_8 "Proposition 8"). During an interview with [MTV News](/wiki/MTV_News "MTV News"), she stated "Why would you put so much money behind something [aimed at] stopping people from loving each other? I just don't understand it".[[479]](#cite_note-LGBTQCommunity-479) In June 2016, Aguilera released single "[Change](/wiki/Change_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Change (Christina Aguilera song)")", which was aimed to raise proceeds to the victim's families and survivors of the [Orlando nightclub shooting](/wiki/Orlando_nightclub_shooting "Orlando nightclub shooting").[[480]](#cite_note-480) During [The X Tour](/wiki/The_X_Tour_(Christina_Aguilera) "The X Tour (Christina Aguilera)") in 2019, Aguilera visited Russia and "blessed" a gay marriage proposal in the backstage, contrary to the [country's laws against "gay propaganda"](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Russia "LGBT rights in Russia").[[481]](#cite_note-481)

In 2024, Aguilera endorsed [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") during her [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election") [presidential campaign](/wiki/Kamala_Harris_2024_presidential_campaign "Kamala Harris 2024 presidential campaign") by performing at the Harris-Walz rally.[[482]](#cite_note-482) In January 2026, Aguilera performed at the [Gala des Pièces Jaunes](/wiki/Op%C3%A9ration_Pi%C3%A8ces_jaunes "Opération Pièces jaunes") in Paris, dedicated to raising funds for young patients and children's hospitals.[[483]](#cite_note-483)

## Personal life

From 2000 to September 2001, Aguilera dated dancer Jorge Santos, whom she met whilst filming one of her music videos.[[484]](#cite_note-484) She began dating music executive Jordan Bratman in late 2002. They got engaged in February 2005.[[485]](#cite_note-485) On November 19, 2005, they married on an estate in [Napa County, California](/wiki/Napa_County,_California "Napa County, California"), holding a private [traditional Jewish](/wiki/Jewish_wedding "Jewish wedding") ceremony.[[486]](#cite_note-486)[[487]](#cite_note-487)[[488]](#cite_note-488) They have a son, Max Liron Bratman, born in 2008.[[489]](#cite_note-489)[[490]](#cite_note-Quihuiz-490) In September 2010, the couple had separated and she filed for divorce in October 2010,[[491]](#cite_note-491) which was finalized in April 2011 and included joint custody of their son.[[492]](#cite_note-492)

In November 2010, Aguilera began dating production assistant Matthew Rutler, whom she met while filming the musical *[Burlesque](/wiki/Burlesque_(2010_American_film) "Burlesque (2010 American film)")*.[[493]](#cite_note-493) Aguilera was arrested for [public intoxication](/wiki/Public_intoxication "Public intoxication") in March 2011, while Rutler was booked for [driving under the influence](/wiki/Driving_under_the_influence "Driving under the influence").[[494]](#cite_note-494) However, the charges against Rutler were dropped the following month.[[495]](#cite_note-495) In 2014, they became engaged on [Valentine's Day](/wiki/Valentine%27s_Day "Valentine's Day").[[496]](#cite_note-496) Later that year, Aguilera gave birth to their daughter, Summer Rain Rutler.[[497]](#cite_note-497)[[490]](#cite_note-Quihuiz-490)

After being raised [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"), she learned about [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") in the mid-2000s.[[498]](#cite_note-498) During 2022, she opened up about [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)") and [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety"), stating "I experienced a lot of trauma in my childhood [...] I've definitely had struggles in the past with depression and anxiety. It's a constant battle to overcome a mind that is anxious, a mind that is always second-guessing".[[499]](#cite_note-499) Aguilera has also been sharing about insecurity and struggling with [body image](/wiki/Body_image "Body image") early in her career.[[500]](#cite_note-500)

In 2007, Aguilera purchased a [Mediterranean-style](/wiki/Mediterranean_Revival_architecture "Mediterranean Revival architecture") mansion in [Beverly Hills](/wiki/Beverly_Hills "Beverly Hills") for $11.5 million, which she sold for $13.5 million in March 2013.[[501]](#cite_note-501) Shortly afterwards, she acquired a house in the [Mulholland Estates](/wiki/Mulholland_Estates "Mulholland Estates")' private enclave, at the [Santa Monica Mountains](/wiki/Santa_Monica_Mountains "Santa Monica Mountains"), for $10 million and currently resides there with her family.[[502]](#cite_note-502)
